topics.title: Engineering Applications of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET755 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engineering Applications of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Engineering Optimization?

**Engineering Optimization** is the systematic, mathematical process of selecting the *best* possible design, operating condition, or strategic decision from a defined set of alternatives, subject to constraints imposed by physical laws, engineering limitations, economic considerations, or safety requirements.

In the precise KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, engineering optimization is defined as:

> **Definition (KTU 2024):** *Optimization is the act of obtaining the best result under given circumstances. In engineering design, it involves maximizing desirable factors (such as efficiency, strength, profit, reliability) and minimizing undesirable ones (such as cost, weight, waste, risk) while satisfying all design, operational, and regulatory constraints.*

Mathematically, every optimization problem has three core components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find:} \quad & \mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T \\
\text{Such that:} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& x_i^{(L)} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{(U)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ = vector of **design variables** (decision variables)
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ = **objective function** to be minimized or maximized
- $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ = **inequality constraints**
- $h_k(\mathbf{x})$ = **equality constraints**
- $x_i^{(L)}, x_i^{(U)}$ = **bounds** (side constraints) on variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The phrase *"Engineering Applications of Optimization"* specifically emphasizes *why* optimization is needed across real engineering domains — design, manufacturing, operations, and resource allocation — and *how* the general mathematical framework translates into domain-specific problem statements.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Hike to the Lake"

Imagine you are standing on a hilly terrain and want to reach the **lowest valley lake** (your optimum) in the *shortest, safest path* (best solution).

- **Your current location** = initial design guess.
- **The altitude of terrain** = objective function value.
- **Your step size and direction** = search strategy (algorithm).
- **Cliffs, fences, private property** = constraints you cannot cross.
- **Reaching the lake** = optimal solution (minimum).

In this analogy:
- If you only walk downhill greedily, you may get stuck in a *shallow pond* (local minimum) instead of finding the *deepest lake* (global minimum). This is precisely the *local vs. global optimum* challenge in optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world mapping:** Engineers don't just want *a* solution — they want the **best feasible** solution under all constraints. Optimization techniques provide the mathematical compass and the step-rule for this journey.

## 1.3 Why is Optimization Essential in Engineering?

Modern engineering problems involve **trade-offs** between competing objectives. Without a formal optimization framework:

1. Designs are based on **trial-and-error** — costly and time-consuming.
2. Performance is often **suboptimal**, leaving money, energy, and material on the table.
3. **Safety margins** are over-engineered, increasing weight and cost.
4. **Multi-disciplinary interactions** (thermal-structural, electrical-mechanical) are ignored.

Optimization transforms engineering from *experience-based design* to **evidence-based, mathematics-driven design**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metric:** Studies by NASA, Boeing, and automotive OEMs report **5%–30% savings in weight, fuel, or cost** simply by applying formal optimization to existing baseline designs — without changing the underlying concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Optimization Process — Structured Steps

Every engineering optimization project, regardless of domain, follows this canonical 6-step protocol:

### Step 1 — Problem Definition
Identify the engineering question. What are we trying to *achieve* (objective) and what is being *controlled* (variables)?

### Step 2 — Data Collection & Modelling
Gather physical constants, material properties, operating ranges, and empirical correlations. Build the analytical model.

### Step 3 — Mathematical Formulation
Translate the engineering problem into the standard form:
- Identify design variables $\mathbf{x}$
- Express objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly
- Express all constraints $g_j, h_k$ as mathematical inequalities/equalities
- Define bounds on each variable

### Step 4 — Selection of Optimization Technique
Choose the algorithm based on:
- Nature of objective (linear vs. nonlinear)
- Nature of variables (continuous vs. discrete)
- Presence of constraints (constrained vs. unconstrained)
- Need for global vs. local optimum

### Step 5 — Solution & Verification
Run the algorithm, obtain the optimum $(\mathbf{x}^*, f^*)$, then verify by re-simulating, prototyping, or sensitivity analysis.

### Step 6 — Implementation & Validation
Deploy the optimal design in the real system and validate against field performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Step 3 (Mathematical Formulation) is the **single most tested step** across all KTU question papers. Always express a problem in standard form before attempting a solution.

## 2.2 Classification of Engineering Optimization Problems

Engineering optimization problems can be classified along several axes:

| Classification Axis | Categories | KTU Exam Hook |
|---|---|---|
| **By Variable Nature** | Continuous / Integer / Mixed-Integer | Identify from variable definitions |
| **By Objective Type** | Single-objective / Multi-objective | Pareto front in multi-objective |
| **By Constraint Type** | Unconstrained / Constrained (equality / inequality) | Lagrange multipliers needed for equality |
| **By Function Type** | Linear Programming (LP) / Nonlinear Programming (NLP) / Quadratic / Geometric | Test for linearity of $f$ and $g_j$ |
| **By Variable Count** | One-dimensional (1D) / Multi-dimensional | Derivative-based vs. direct methods |
| **By Determinism** | Deterministic / Stochastic (under uncertainty) | Expectation / robust formulation |
| **By Feasibility Region** | Convex / Non-convex | Guarantees global vs. local optimum |

## 2.3 Common Engineering Application Domains

### 🏗️ **Civil & Structural Engineering**
- Minimum weight truss design subject to stress/displacement limits
- Optimal routing of pipelines, roads, and transmission lines
- Optimal mix design of concrete (cement, aggregate, water proportions)
- Scheduling of construction projects (PERT/CPM)

### ⚙️ **Mechanical Engineering**
- Minimum weight machine components (shafts, springs, pressure vessels)
- Optimal gear train ratios for minimum wear / maximum efficiency
- Optimal cutting parameters in machining (speed, feed, depth)
- Thermal system optimization (heat exchanger sizing)
- Vehicle suspension tuning, aerodynamic shape optimization

### ⚡ **Electrical Engineering**
- Optimal power flow in grids
- Optimal placement and sizing of distributed generation
- Antenna design, filter design (minimizing stopband ripple)
- Economic dispatch of generators

### 🏭 **Industrial / Production Engineering**
- Inventory control (EOQ models)
- Production planning, scheduling, and sequencing
- Assembly line balancing
- Supply chain network optimization

### 🧪 **Chemical Engineering**
- Optimal reactor design (maximizing conversion/yield)
- Distillation column optimization
- Optimal pipeline diameter for minimum cost
- Process flow-sheet synthesis

### ✈️ **Aerospace Engineering**
- Minimum weight wing structure
- Optimal trajectory planning (minimum fuel)
- Re-entry trajectory optimization
- Satellite constellation placement

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Expression | Meaning | Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ | Standard minimization form | All problems |
| $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; j=1,\dots,m$ | Inequality constraints | Constrained problems |
| $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; k=1,\dots,p$ | Equality constraints | Constrained problems |
| $x_i^{(L)} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{(U)}$ | Side (bound) constraints | Bounded variables |
| $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | First-order necessary optimality condition (unconstrained) | Smooth objectives |
| $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$ | Second-order sufficient condition (positive definite Hessian) | Local minimum guarantee |
| $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_k h_k(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \mu_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$ | Lagrangian (with KKT multipliers) | Constrained NLP |
| $\partial L / \partial \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ | KKT stationarity | Constrained NLP |
| $\mu_j g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Complementary slackness | KKT conditions |
| $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | Linear objective | LP problems |
| $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$ | Linear constraint matrix form | LP / QP |
| $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | Quadratic objective | QP problems |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the KTU board exam, students frequently confuse *minimization* and *maximization* in the standard form. Always convert a maximization to a minimization form: $\max f(\mathbf{x}) = -\min [-f(\mathbf{x})]$ before applying the formula sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Minimum Cost Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

A classic KTU-style engineering optimization problem is the **minimum-cost cylindrical pressure vessel** with closed hemispherical ends.

### Problem Statement
A cylindrical pressure vessel of volume **V** must be designed. Find the dimensions that **minimize the total cost** of material, given:
- Cost per unit area of the cylindrical wall = $C_1$
- Cost per unit area of the hemispherical cap = $C_2$

### Step 1 — Identify Design Variables
We choose radius $r$ and height $h$ (of the cylindrical section) as design variables:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ h \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Volume Constraint (Equality)
Total volume is the sum of cylinder + two hemispheres (= one full sphere):

$$
V = \pi r^2 h + \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Surface Areas
Surface area of cylindrical wall:

$$
A_{cyl} = 2\pi r h
$$

Surface area of two hemispherical ends (= one full sphere):

$$
A_{sph} = 4\pi r^2
$$

### Step 4 — Formulate the Cost Objective

$$
\text{Cost} = C_1 (2\pi r h) + C_2 (4\pi r^2)
$$

$$
f(r,h) = 2\pi C_1 r h + 4\pi C_2 r^2
$$

### Step 5 — Standard Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize:} \quad & f(r,h) = 2\pi C_1 r h + 4\pi C_2 r^2 \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad & h_1(r,h) = \pi r^2 h + \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 - V = 0 \\
& r > 0, \quad h > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Eliminate Variable Using the Equality Constraint
From the volume equation, express $h$ in terms of $r$:

$$
h = \frac{V - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{\pi r^2}
$$

### Step 7 — Substitute into the Objective
Let $K_1 = 2\pi C_1$ and $K_2 = 4\pi C_2$:

$$
f(r) = K_1 \cdot r \cdot \frac{V - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{\pi r^2} + K_2 r^2
$$

Simplify term by term:

$$
f(r) = \frac{K_1}{\pi r}\left(V - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\right) + K_2 r^2
$$

$$
f(r) = \frac{K_1 V}{\pi r} - \frac{4 K_1 r^2}{3} + K_2 r^2
$$

$$
f(r) = \frac{K_1 V}{\pi r} + \left(K_2 - \frac{4K_1}{3}\right) r^2
$$

### Step 8 — Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$
\frac{df(r)}{dr} = -\frac{K_1 V}{\pi r^2} + 2\left(K_2 - \frac{4K_1}{3}\right) r = 0
$$

### Step 9 — Solve for $r^*$

$$
2\left(K_2 - \frac{4K_1}{3}\right) r^3 = \frac{K_1 V}{\pi}
$$

$$
r^3 = \frac{K_1 V}{2\pi \left(K_2 - \frac{4K_1}{3}\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, r^* = \left[\frac{K_1 V}{2\pi \left(K_2 - \frac{4K_1}{3}\right)}\right]^{1/3} \,}
$$

### Step 10 — Back-Substitute to Find $h^*$

$$
h^* = \frac{V - \frac{4}{3}\pi (r^*)^3}{\pi (r^*)^2}
$$

### Step 11 — Verify via Second-Derivative Test
Compute the second derivative:

$$
\frac{d^2 f}{dr^2} = \frac{2 K_1 V}{\pi r^3} + 2\left(K_2 - \frac{4K_1}{3}\right)
$$

At $r = r^*$, both terms are positive (assuming $K_2 > \frac{4K_1}{3}$, which holds for practical cost ratios). Hence the second-derivative test confirms a **local minimum**, and convexity guarantees it is the **global minimum**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Formulation Matrix — Engineering Applications

| Engineering Domain | Design Variables $\mathbf{x}$ | Objective $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Constraints $g_j, h_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Truss weight minimization | Cross-section areas $A_i$ | Total weight $W = \sum \rho_i A_i L_i$ | Stress limits $\sigma \leq \sigma_{allow}$; displacement $\delta \leq \delta_{max}$ |
| Economic dispatch (Power) | Generator outputs $P_i$ | Total fuel cost $\sum F_i(P_i)$ | Power balance $\sum P_i = P_D + P_L$; generator limits $P_i^{\min} \leq P_i \leq P_i^{\max}$ |
| EOQ Inventory | Order quantity $Q$ | Annual cost = ordering + holding | Demand satisfaction |
| Concrete mix design | $x_1$=cement, $x_2$=water, $x_3$=aggregate | Compressive strength (maximize) | Water-cement ratio limits; workability; total volume = 1 m³ |
| Optimal pipe diameter | Pipe diameter $D$ | Annual cost = pump + depreciation | Flow rate $Q$; pressure drop $\Delta P \leq \Delta P_{allow}$ |
| Machining parameter | Speed $v$, feed $f$ | Production cost per piece | Tool life, surface finish, machine power limits |
| Heat exchanger | Area $A$, flow rate $\dot{m}$ | Total annualized cost | Heat duty $Q = UA \Delta T_{LMTD}$; pressure drops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always *restate the problem in the standard three-component form* (objective + equality/inequality constraints + bounds) before applying any algorithm. Examiners award **1–2 marks** purely for the correct formulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Engineering Optimization Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the entire optimization lifecycle — from problem identification through deployment — as it is taught in the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Engineering Problem] --> B[Gather Data and Physical Constants]
    B --> C[Identify Design Variables x1 x2 xn]
    C --> D[Formulate Objective Function f of x]
    D --> E[Express Constraints gj of x and hk of x]
    E --> F{Linear or Nonlinear?}
    F -->|Linear| G[Apply LP or Simplex Method]
    F -->|Nonlinear| H{Constrained?}
    H -->|Unconstrained| I[Apply Gradient Based or Direct Search]
    H -->|Constrained| J[Apply KKT or Penalty Methods]
    G --> K[Obtain Optimal Solution x star and f star]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Verify via Sensitivity Analysis]
    L --> M[Implement and Validate in Real System]
    M --> N[Final Optimized Engineering Design]
```

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMET755]`
**Define engineering optimization. List any four real-world engineering applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Engineering Optimization** is the mathematical discipline of finding the best (minimum or maximum) value of an objective function subject to a set of constraints.

The general standard form is:

$$
\min \; f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0
$$

**Four engineering applications:**

1. **Minimum weight structural design** of trusses, beams, and machine components.
2. **Optimal economic dispatch** of electrical power generators.
3. **Optimal cutting parameters** (speed, feed, depth of cut) in machining.
4. **Optimal pipe diameter** selection for minimum annual pumping cost in process plants.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper PEMET755]`
**Differentiate between local optimum and global optimum with a suitable sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

- A **local optimum** is a feasible point where the objective function value is better than all points in its immediate neighborhood, but not necessarily the best over the entire feasible region.

- A **global optimum** is the absolutely best feasible point over the entire feasible region.

```
f(x)
 ^
 |      *  local min
 |    __/ \__
 |   /   ^   \         * global min
 |  /    |    \      __/
 | /     |     \    /
 |/______|______\__/
 |       x*     x_global
 +-----------------> x
```

For convex problems, every local optimum is also a global optimum. For non-convex problems (multi-modal functions), gradient-based methods may get trapped in local optima — motivating the use of global search techniques (genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, particle swarm).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMET755]`

**A cylindrical pressure vessel with hemispherical ends must store a volume $V = 10 \; m^3$. The cost of the cylindrical wall is $C_1 = 150$ Rs/m² and the cost of the hemispherical end caps is $C_2 = 200$ Rs/m².**

**(a) Formulate the optimization problem in standard form.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Solve for the optimal radius $r^*$ and height $h^*$ that minimize the total cost.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Formulation (7 marks)**

**Design Variables:**

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ h \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Objective Function (Total Cost):**

$$
\min \; f(r, h) = 2\pi C_1 r h + 4\pi C_2 r^2
$$

Substituting $C_1 = 150$ and $C_2 = 200$:

$$
f(r, h) = 300\pi r h + 800\pi r^2
$$

**Equality Constraint (Fixed Volume):**

$$
h_1(r,h) = \pi r^2 h + \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 - 10 = 0
$$

**Side Constraints:**

$$
r > 0, \quad h > 0
$$

**Standard Form Block Summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize:} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = 300\pi r h + 800\pi r^2 \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad & h(\mathbf{x}) = \pi r^2 h + \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 - 10 = 0 \\
& r, h > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Eliminate $h$ using the equality constraint:**

$$
\pi r^2 h = 10 - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \implies h = \frac{10 - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $f$:**

$$
f(r) = 300\pi r \cdot \frac{10 - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{\pi r^2} + 800\pi r^2
$$

$$
f(r) = \frac{300 \cdot 10}{r} - 400\pi r^2 + 800\pi r^2
$$

$$
f(r) = \frac{3000}{r} + 400\pi r^2
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate:**

$$
\frac{df}{dr} = -\frac{3000}{r^2} + 800\pi r = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $r^*$:**

$$
800\pi r^3 = 3000
$$

$$
r^3 = \frac{3000}{800\pi} = \frac{15}{4\pi} = \frac{15}{12.566}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, r^* \approx 1.060 \; \text{m} \,}
$$

**Step 5 — Back-substitute for $h^*$:**

$$
h^* = \frac{10 - \frac{4}{3}\pi (1.060)^3}{\pi (1.060)^2}
$$

Numerically:

$$
h^* \approx \frac{10 - 4.984}{3.529} \approx \frac{5.016}{3.529} \approx 1.421 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify Minimum:**

$$
\frac{d^2 f}{dr^2} = \frac{6000}{r^3} + 800\pi > 0 \quad \forall r > 0
$$

So $f(r)$ is convex, and $r^*$ is a **global minimum**.

**Final Answer:**

$$
r^* \approx 1.060 \; \text{m}, \quad h^* \approx 1.421 \; \text{m}, \quad f^* \approx 4241 \; \text{Rs}
$$

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper PEMET755]`

**A manufacturing firm produces two products P₁ and P₂. Each unit of P₁ requires 2 hours of machining and 1 hour of assembly, yielding a profit of Rs. 30. Each unit of P₂ requires 1 hour of machining and 3 hours of assembly, yielding a profit of Rs. 40. The available machining hours per day = 100, assembly hours per day = 120. Demand forecasts indicate that at least 10 units of P₁ and 20 units of P₂ must be produced daily.**

**(a) Formulate the Linear Programming (LP) problem in standard form.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss how this problem can be solved graphically and interpret the feasible region.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) LP Formulation (7 marks)**

**Decision Variables:**

$$
x_1 = \text{number of units of } P_1 \text{ produced per day}
$$

$$
x_2 = \text{number of units of } P_2 \text{ produced per day}
$$

**Objective Function (Maximize Profit):**

$$
\max \; Z = 30 x_1 + 40 x_2
$$

Equivalent minimization form: $\min -Z = -30x_1 - 40x_2$.

**Constraints:**

*Machining capacity:*

$$
2x_1 + x_2 \leq 100
$$

*Assembly capacity:*

$$
x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 120
$$

*Demand minima:*

$$
x_1 \geq 10 \implies -x_1 \leq -10
$$

$$
x_2 \geq 20 \implies -x_2 \leq -20
$$

*Non-negativity (redundant given demand constraints):*

$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

**Standard LP Form Block:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \; Z = 30 x_1 + 40 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 100 \\
x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 120 \\
x_1 \geq 10, \quad x_2 \geq 20
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Graphical Solution (7 marks)**

Plot the constraint lines in the $x_1$–$x_2$ plane:

- Line A: $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ passes through $(50,0)$ and $(0,100)$.
- Line B: $x_1 + 3x_2 = 120$ passes through $(120,0)$ and $(0,40)$.
- Line C: $x_1 = 10$ (vertical line).
- Line D: $x_2 = 20$ (horizontal line).

The **feasible region** is the polygon bounded by the intersection of all constraints. The corner points (vertices) are evaluated as follows:

| Vertex $(x_1, x_2)$ | Computation | $Z = 30x_1 + 40x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $(10, 20)$ | Demand minimum | $300 + 800 = 1100$ |
| $(40, 20)$ | $2x_1 + x_2 = 100, \; x_2 = 20$ | $1200 + 800 = 2000$ |
| $(36, 28)$ | Intersection of $2x_1+x_2=100$ and $x_1+3x_2=120$ | $1080 + 1120 = 2200$ |
| $(10, 36.67)$ | $x_1+3x_2=120, \; x_1=10$ | $300 + 1466.67 = 1766.67$ |

**Optimal Solution:**

$$
\boxed{\, x_1^* = 36, \quad x_2^* = 28, \quad Z^* = \text{Rs. } 2200 \,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Producing 36 units of P₁ and 28 units of P₂ per day utilizes resources fully and yields maximum daily profit of Rs. 2200.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Sign of inequality on conversion:** When converting $x \geq a$ to standard form $\leq$, students often write the wrong direction. Use $-x \leq -a$.
> 2. **Demand constraints must be retained** — many students ignore them, expanding the feasible region incorrectly.
> 3. **Feasible region must be shaded** clearly on the graph, not implied.
> 4. **Vertex evaluation** must be shown in a tabular format — examiners specifically award 1 mark for the tabulation step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Engineering Applications of Optimization**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Optimization = finding the *best* feasible solution under constraints.
- 🔹 **Standard Form Components:** Objective $f(\mathbf{x})$, inequality constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, equality constraints $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, bounds on variables.
- 🔹 **Always formulate first, then solve.** Formulation carries 50% of the marks in KTU problems.
- 🔹 **Design variables** are quantities the engineer controls (geometry, material, operating setpoints).
- 🔹 **Constraints** arise from physics, safety, cost, regulations, or manufacturing limits.
- 🔹 **Classification axes** to remember: continuous vs. discrete, linear vs. nonlinear, single vs. multi-objective, constrained vs. unconstrained, deterministic vs. stochastic, convex vs. non-convex.
- 🔹 **Local vs. Global Optimum:** A local optimum is best in its neighborhood; a global optimum is best everywhere. Convexity guarantees local = global.
- 🔹 **Optimality conditions** (unconstrained smooth case): $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ and $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$.
- 🔹 **KKT conditions** are the constrained generalization of the stationary condition.
- 🔹 **Engineering domains:** Civil (truss), Mechanical (machining, weight), Electrical (dispatch), Chemical (reactor), Industrial (EOQ), Aerospace (trajectory).
- 🔹 **Problem-solving template:** Identify variables → Write objective → Express constraints → Standard form → Solve → Verify second-order condition → Interpret result.
- 🔹 **Pressure vessel problem** is the *single most repeated* KTU numerical — practice it.
- 🔹 **Production planning LP** is the *single most repeated* 14-mark KTU formulation question.
- 🔹 **Convert max to min** before applying standard minimization algorithms: $\max f = -\min(-f)$.
- 🔹 **Always state units** of design variables and objective in your final answer — examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- 🔹 **Convexity = guarantee** of global optimality for smooth unconstrained problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulation of Design Problems as Mathematical Programming Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET755 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Formulation of Design Problems as Mathematical Programming Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mathematical Programming Problem (MPP)** — also called a **Mathematical Optimization Problem** — is the formal mathematical representation of a real-world decision-making scenario in which we seek to determine the values of controllable inputs (called **decision variables**) that optimize a prescribed performance criterion (the **objective function**) while satisfying a set of physical, economic, or operational restrictions (the **constraints**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Form):**
> A general Mathematical Programming Problem is expressed as:
> $$\text{Find } \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$
> $$\text{that } \textbf{minimizes (or maximizes) } f(\mathbf{x})$$
> $$\text{subject to } g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$
> $$\text{and } x_i \ge 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
> Here $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**, $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ are the **constraint functions**, and $b_j$ are the **right-hand side (RHS) constants**.

The term *"programming"* here does **not** refer to computer programming; it originates from the military term *"mathematical programming"*, meaning the planning (programming) of operations using mathematics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Factory Chef"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Smart Factory Chef** 🍳
> Imagine you are a head chef in a factory-kitchen with a fixed budget, limited ingredients in the pantry, and a maximum oven capacity. You must decide **how many units of each dish to prepare today** so that the **total profit is maximized**. The decision variables are the number of each dish, the objective function is total profit, and the constraints are the pantry limits and oven capacity. A Mathematical Programming Problem is exactly this — a chef's recipe book written in the language of algebra.

## 1.3 Why Formulation Matters in Engineering Design

In engineering, every design decision — selecting the cross-section of a beam, choosing a pipe diameter, sizing a spring, or planning a power system — is fundamentally a constrained optimization problem. **Formulation** is the critical first step that converts a verbal design brief into a solvable mathematical model. A poor formulation leads to meaningless or infeasible solutions, regardless of the elegance of the solution algorithm.

> [!TIP]
> **Core Insight:** The power of a Mathematical Programming Problem lies not in its complexity, but in the clarity with which it captures the *essence* of the design trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Essential Components of a Mathematical Programming Problem

Every MPP — linear, nonlinear, integer, or otherwise — is built from exactly **four components**. The KTU examiner will always test your ability to identify these in any problem statement.

### Component 1: Decision Variables ($x_i$)
These are the **controllable, independent quantities** that the designer/engineer is free to choose. The set of all decision variables forms the **decision vector** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** In a diet problem, $x_1$ = kg of rice, $x_2$ = kg of dal. In a beam design, $x_1$ = width $b$ (mm), $x_2$ = depth $d$ (mm).

### Component 2: Objective Function ($f(\mathbf{x})$)
A **scalar measure of performance** that the decision-maker wishes to optimize. The two standard optimization senses are:
- **Minimize** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — e.g., cost, weight, time, error
- **Maximize** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — e.g., profit, strength, efficiency, life

### Component 3: Constraints ($g_j(\mathbf{x})$)
**Restrictions on the design** arising from physics, materials, regulations, budget, or operational limits. The KTU standard classification is:

| Constraint Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Inequality ($\le$) | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le b_j$ | Resource limits (e.g., budget) |
| Inequality ($\ge$) | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \ge b_j$ | Minimum requirements (e.g., strength) |
| Equality ($=$) | $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = c_k$ | Design specifications (e.g., balance) |
| Bound | $x_i \ge 0$ or $L_i \le x_i \le U_i$ | Physical non-negativity / range |

### Component 4: Parameters ($b_j, c_j$)
**Fixed numerical constants** that quantify the constraints and objective. They are *given* and are not decision variables.

## 2.2 Classification of Mathematical Programming Problems (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification is a guaranteed 3-mark KTU question.** Memorize the table below.

| Class | Acronym | Key Property of $f$ and $g$ | Typical Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Programming Problem** | LPP | Both $f$ and all $g_j$ are **linear** in $x_i$ | Product mix, transportation, blending |
| **Nonlinear Programming Problem** | NLPP | At least one of $f$ or $g_j$ is **nonlinear** | Spring design, reactor yield |
| **Integer Programming Problem** | IPP | All $x_i$ restricted to **integers** | Project selection, scheduling |
| **Mixed-Integer NLP** | MINLP | Some $x_i$ integer, rest continuous | Process network design |
| **Quadratic Programming Problem** | QPP | $f$ is **quadratic**, $g_j$ linear | Portfolio optimization |
| **Geometric Programming Problem** | GPP | $f$ and $g_j$ are **posynomials** | Engineering design synthesis |
| **Dynamic Programming Problem** | DPP | Problem decomposed into **stages** | Inventory, shortest path |

## 2.3 The Canonical Standard Form (KTU Board Format)

For every MPP, the KTU board expects it to be rewritten in **canonical standard form** before solving. The two canonical forms are:

### Standard Form I — Minimization with $\le$ constraints
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Minimize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& x_i \ge 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}$$

### Standard Form II — Maximization with $\ge$ constraints
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Maximize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \ge b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& x_i \ge 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Rule:** Any "$\ge$" inequality can be converted to "$\le$" by multiplying both sides by $-1$. Any "$\ge$" objective can be converted to "$\le$" by $\text{Min } f = -\text{Max}(-f)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mathematical Programming formulation is the **backbone of modern engineering design optimization** in:
- **Structural Engineering:** Minimum-weight truss and beam design subject to stress limits.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Optimal spring, gear, and bearing design.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Reactor design maximizing conversion subject to equilibrium constraints.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Economic dispatch minimizing fuel cost subject to power balance.
- **Civil Engineering:** Project scheduling and resource allocation under budget caps.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ | Decision Vector | Independent design variables |
| $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Objective Function | Scalar criterion to optimize |
| $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le b_j$ | Functional Constraint | Operational/physical limits |
| $x_i \ge 0$ | Non-negativity Constraint | Sign restriction |
| $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Equality Constraint | Design specification |
| $S$ | Feasible Region | Set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying all constraints |
| $\mathbf{x}^{\star}$ | Optimal Solution | Best feasible $\mathbf{x}$ |
| $f(\mathbf{x}^{\star})$ | Optimal Value | Objective at optimum |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Universal Six-Step Formulation Procedure

The KTU board marks full credit only when the formulation is presented in a **structured, six-step format**. Follow this template exactly:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-1:** Read the problem and list all unknown quantities that the designer can control. These are the **decision variables** $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
> **Step-2:** Express the design objective (cost, profit, weight, etc.) mathematically as $f(\mathbf{x})$.
> **Step-3:** Identify every physical, economic, or operational restriction. Write them as constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le / \ge / = b_j$.
> **Step-4:** Apply sign restrictions $x_i \ge 0$ (and any upper bounds).
> **Step-5:** Combine all four components into a single MPP.
> **Step-6:** Classify the resulting MPP (LPP, NLPP, IPP, etc.).

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Product-Mix LPP (Manufacturing)

**Problem Statement:** A small factory produces two products, $P_1$ and $P_2$. Each unit of $P_1$ requires **2 hours** of machine time and **1 hour** of labor, yielding a profit of **₹30**. Each unit of $P_2$ requires **1 hour** of machine time and **3 hours** of labor, yielding a profit of **₹20**. The factory has at most **100 machine hours** and **90 labor hours** available per day. Formulate the problem of maximizing profit as an LPP.

### Step-1: Define Decision Variables
Let $x_1$ = number of units of $P_1$ produced per day, $x_2$ = number of units of $P_2$ produced per day.

### Step-2: Formulate the Objective Function
Total daily profit $Z$ in rupees:
$$Z = 30 x_1 + 20 x_2$$
The designer's goal is to **maximize** $Z$.

### Step-3: Formulate the Constraints
Machine-time constraint (at most 100 hours):
$$2 x_1 + 1 x_2 \le 100$$
Labor-time constraint (at most 90 hours):
$$1 x_1 + 3 x_2 \le 90$$

### Step-4: Non-Negativity
$$x_1 \ge 0, \quad x_2 \ge 0$$
(Physical sense: one cannot produce a negative number of items.)

### Step-5: Combine into Standard Form
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Maximize } \quad & Z = 30 x_1 + 20 x_2 \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & 2 x_1 + x_2 \le 100 \\
& x_1 + 3 x_2 \le 90 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step-6: Classification
Since $f$ and all $g_j$ are **linear** in $x_1, x_2$, this is a **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** with 2 variables and 2 constraints.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Diet Problem (LPP)

**Problem Statement:** A dietitian must design a daily meal using two foods, $F_1$ and $F_2$. Each kg of $F_1$ provides 4 units of Vitamin A and 2 units of Vitamin B, costing ₹6. Each kg of $F_2$ provides 3 units of Vitamin A and 4 units of Vitamin B, costing ₹10. The minimum daily requirement is **20 units of Vitamin A** and **16 units of Vitamin B**. Formulate the cost-minimization problem.

### Step-1: Decision Variables
$x_1$ = kg of food $F_1$ per day; $x_2$ = kg of food $F_2$ per day.

### Step-2: Objective Function
Total daily cost $C$ in rupees:
$$C = 6 x_1 + 10 x_2 \quad \text{(to be minimized)}$$

### Step-3: Constraints
Vitamin A requirement ($\ge 20$ units):
$$4 x_1 + 3 x_2 \ge 20$$
Vitamin B requirement ($\ge 16$ units):
$$2 x_1 + 4 x_2 \ge 16$$

### Step-4: Non-Negativity
$$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

### Step-5: Standard Form
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Minimize } \quad & C = 6 x_1 + 10 x_2 \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & 4 x_1 + 3 x_2 \ge 20 \\
& 2 x_1 + 4 x_2 \ge 16 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step-6: Classification
**Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** — minimization sense with $\ge$ constraints.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Engineering Design (Nonlinear Formulation)

**Problem Statement:** A cylindrical pressure vessel of internal volume $V = 1 \text{ m}^3$ must be designed. The top and bottom are hemispherical. The material cost is proportional to the surface area, with the cylindrical wall costing ₹500/m² and the hemispherical caps costing ₹600/m². Formulate the problem of minimizing total material cost as a Mathematical Programming Problem.

### Step-1: Decision Variables
Let $r$ = internal radius (m), $h$ = height of cylindrical section (m).

### Step-2: Geometric Relations
Total volume of vessel (cylinder + two hemispheres = one full sphere):
$$V = \pi r^2 h + \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = 1$$

### Step-3: Surface Area Components
Cylindrical wall area (in m²):
$$A_c = 2 \pi r h$$
Two hemispherical caps together form one full sphere of area:
$$A_s = 4 \pi r^2$$

### Step-4: Objective Function
Total cost $C$ in rupees:
$$C = 500 \cdot (2 \pi r h) + 600 \cdot (4 \pi r^2) = 1000 \pi r h + 2400 \pi r^2$$

### Step-5: Standard Form
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Minimize } \quad & C(r, h) = 1000 \pi r h + 2400 \pi r^2 \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & \pi r^2 h + \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = 1 \\
& r, h \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step-6: Classification
The objective contains a **bilinear term** $r h$ and a **quadratic term** $r^2$. Hence, this is a **Nonlinear Programming Problem (NLPP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Nonlinear?** A product of two decision variables ($r \cdot h$) makes the function non-linear. If we had treated $rh$ as a single variable (e.g., let $y = rh$), the problem would reduce to an LPP — a common modeling trick in KTU problems.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Spring Design (Classical KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A helical compression spring must be designed. Let $d$ = wire diameter (mm), $D$ = mean coil diameter (mm), $N$ = number of active coils. The constraints are: (i) shear stress $\tau \le \tau_{\max}$, (ii) deflection $\delta \le \delta_{\max}$, (iii) geometric bound $D/d \ge 4$. Formulate the problem of minimizing the spring weight.

### Step-1: Decision Variables
$x_1 = d$, $x_2 = D$, $x_3 = N$.

### Step-2: Objective Function
Spring weight is proportional to its volume $V_s$:
$$V_s = \frac{\pi^2 d^2 D N}{4}$$
Minimize weight $\equiv$ Minimize $V_s$.

### Step-3: Constraints (typical)
$$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{8 P D}{\pi d^3} \le \tau_{\max} \quad \text{(shear stress)} \\
& \frac{8 P D^3 N}{G d^4} \le \delta_{\max} \quad \text{(deflection)} \\
& D - 4 d \ge 0 \quad \text{(geometric)} \\
& d, D, N \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step-4: Classification
Contains powers of variables and products — clearly an **NLPP** (in fact, a **Geometric Programming** candidate after suitable transformation).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Formulation Template

| Engineering Scenario | Typical Decision Variables | Typical Objective | Typical Constraints | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Product mix | Units of each product | Maximize profit | Raw material, labor, machine | LPP |
| Diet / Blending | Kg of each ingredient | Minimize cost | Nutritional requirements | LPP |
| Transportation | Units shipped from $i$ to $j$ | Minimize transport cost | Supply, demand | LPP |
| Pressure vessel | Radius, height | Minimize surface/cost | Volume, thickness | NLPP |
| Truss/Beam | Cross-section dimensions | Minimize weight | Stress, deflection, buckling | NLPP |
| Project selection | 0/1 selection variables | Maximize NPV | Budget, manpower | IPP |
| Power dispatch | Generator outputs | Minimize fuel cost | Demand, generator limits | LPP / QPP |
| Spring design | $d, D, N$ | Minimize weight/volume | Stress, deflection, geometry | NLPP / GPP |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Step Formulation Flowchart

The diagram below captures the exact logical sequence a student must follow when converting a verbal engineering problem into a formal Mathematical Programming Problem. This is the highest-yield conceptual flow in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Read Verbal Problem Statement] --> B[Step 2: Identify Decision Variables x1 x2 ... xn]
    B --> C[Step 3: Express Objective Function fx]
    C --> D{Minimize or Maximize}
    D --> E[Step 4: List All Physical Economic Operational Limits]
    E --> F[Step 5: Convert Each Limit into Constraint gjx rel bj]
    F --> G[Step 6: Add Non Negativity Bounds xi ge 0]
    G --> H[Combine into Canonical Standard Form]
    H --> I{Linear or Nonlinear}
    I -->|All linear| J[Linear Programming Problem LPP]
    I -->|Any nonlinear| K[Nonlinear Programming Problem NLPP]
    I -->|All integer| L[Integer Programming Problem IPP]
    I -->|Mixed| M[MINLP or QPP or GPP]
    J --> N[Ready for Solver: Graphical / Simplex / KKT]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MPP

This block diagram shows the *interactions* between the four fundamental components of any Mathematical Programming Problem, the same structure the KTU examiner expects you to label in descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        DV[Decision Variables x1 x2 ... xn]
        PAR[Parameters b1 b2 ... bm]
    end
    subgraph CORE[OPTIMIZATION CORE]
        OBJ[Objective Function fx]
        CON[Constraint Functions g1x g2x ... gmx]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        OPT[Optimal Solution x star]
        VAL[Optimal Value fx star]
    end
    DV --> OBJ
    DV --> CON
    PAR --> CON
    OBJ --> OPT
    CON --> OPT
    OPT --> VAL
    CON -.Feasibility Check.-> OPT
    OBJ -.Optimality Check.-> OPT
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of MPP Classification

A hierarchical view of how the broad family of Mathematical Programming Problems branches from the general formulation. KTU frequently asks: *"Classify the following problem."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[General Mathematical Programming Problem MPP]
    ROOT --> A1{All functions linear?}
    ROOT --> A2{All variables integer?}
    ROOT --> A3{Polynomial posynomial form?}
    A1 -->|Yes| LPP[Linear Programming Problem LPP]
    A1 -->|No| NLPP[Nonlinear Programming Problem NLPP]
    A2 -->|Yes and linear| IPP[Integer Programming Problem IPP]
    A2 -->|Yes and nonlinear| INLP[Integer Nonlinear Programming INLP]
    A2 -->|Mixed| MINLP[Mixed Integer Nonlinear MINLP]
    A3 -->|Yes| GPP[Geometric Programming Problem GPP]
    LPP -->|Quadratic f| QPP[Quadratic Programming QPP]
    NLPP -->|Unconstrained| UNC[Unconstrained NLP]
    NLPP -->|Constrained| CONS[Constrained NLP]
```

## 4.4 Anatomy of a Single Constraint

The KTU board often asks students to *"interpret the constraint"* in plain language. The diagram below maps each mathematical symbol of a typical constraint to its physical or engineering meaning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Constraint g1x1 x2 le 100] --> B[g1 = Constraint Function]
    A --> C[x1 x2 = Decision Variables]
    A --> D[100 = Right Hand Side Constant]
    B --> E[Represents Usage of a Resource]
    C --> F[Controllable Design Quantities]
    D --> G[Represents Available Capacity]
    E --> H[Engineering Interpretation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[The Total Resource Consumption Must Not Exceed 100 Units]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
> **Define a Mathematical Programming Problem. List its four essential components with one-line descriptions.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):
A **Mathematical Programming Problem (MPP)** is a formal mathematical model used to find the optimal values of controllable decision variables that optimize a given objective, while satisfying a finite set of constraints.

The four essential components are:

| # | Component | One-line Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Decision Variables** ($x_i$) | Controllable independent quantities to be determined |
| 2 | **Objective Function** ($f(\mathbf{x})$) | Scalar performance criterion to be minimized or maximized |
| 3 | **Constraints** ($g_j(\mathbf{x})$) | Restrictions arising from physical, economic, or operational limits |
| 4 | **Parameters** ($b_j, c_j$) | Fixed given numerical constants |

*(1 mark for the definition + 2 marks for the four components in a structured table.)*

---

> **Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
> **Differentiate between a Linear Programming Problem (LPP) and a Nonlinear Programming Problem (NLPP) with one engineering example each.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | LPP | NLPP |
|---|---|---|
| Objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Linear in $x_i$ | Contains nonlinear terms (powers, products, trig) |
| Constraint functions $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ | All linear | At least one nonlinear |
| Feasible region | Convex polyhedron | May be non-convex |
| Standard solver | Simplex Method | KKT conditions, gradient methods |
| **Engineering Example** | Product mix in a factory | Minimum-weight pressure vessel design |

*(1 mark each for the three distinguishing points + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** A company manufactures two products $A$ and $B$. Each unit of $A$ requires 3 hours of machining and 2 hours of finishing, giving a profit of ₹50. Each unit of $B$ requires 2 hours of machining and 4 hours of finishing, giving a profit of ₹60. The available machining hours per week are 240 and finishing hours are 320. Formulate this as a Linear Programming Problem (LPP) in standard form and classify it.

### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Decision Variables [1 mark]:**
Let $x_1$ = number of units of product $A$ produced per week.
Let $x_2$ = number of units of product $B$ produced per week.

**Step 2 — Objective Function [2 marks]:**
Total weekly profit $Z$ in rupees:
$$Z = 50 x_1 + 60 x_2$$
Goal: **Maximize** $Z$.

**Step 3 — Constraints [2 marks]:**
Machining hours ($\le 240$):
$$3 x_1 + 2 x_2 \le 240$$
Finishing hours ($\le 320$):
$$2 x_1 + 4 x_2 \le 320$$

**Step 4 — Non-negativity [1 mark]:**
$$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

**Step 5 — Standard Form LPP [1 mark]:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Maximize } \quad & Z = 50 x_1 + 60 x_2 \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & 3 x_1 + 2 x_2 \le 240 \\
& 2 x_1 + 4 x_2 \le 320 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Classification:** **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** since both $f$ and all constraints are linear in $x_1, x_2$.

---

> **Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** A rectangular open-top box is to be constructed using a fixed area $A$ of sheet metal. The volume of the box must be at least $V_0$. Formulate the problem of minimizing the surface area as a Mathematical Programming Problem and classify it. State all four components explicitly.

### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Decision Variables [1 mark]:**
Let $x_1 = l$ = length (m), $x_2 = w$ = width (m), $x_3 = h$ = height (m) of the open-top box.

**Step 2 — Objective Function [1.5 marks]:**
Surface area (bottom + 4 sides, no top):
$$A_s = l w + 2 l h + 2 w h = x_1 x_2 + 2 x_1 x_3 + 2 x_2 x_3$$
Goal: **Minimize** $A_s$.

**Step 3 — Constraints [2 marks]:**
Volume requirement (volume must be at least $V_0$):
$$l w h \ge V_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 x_2 x_3 \ge V_0$$
Material availability (no constraint mentioned — none, or we can use it as $A_s \le A$ if specified):
$$A_s \le A \quad \text{(only if } A \text{ is a cap, otherwise omitted)}$$

**Step 4 — Non-negativity [1 mark]:**
$$x_1, x_2, x_3 \ge 0$$

**Step 5 — Standard Form [1 mark]:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Minimize } \quad & A_s = x_1 x_2 + 2 x_1 x_3 + 2 x_2 x_3 \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & x_1 x_2 x_3 \ge V_0 \\
& x_1, x_2, x_3 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Classification [0.5 marks]:**
The objective contains **product terms** $x_i x_j$ and the constraint contains a **triple product** $x_1 x_2 x_3$. Both are **nonlinear** in the decision variables. Hence, this is a **Nonlinear Programming Problem (NLPP)**.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** A farmer has a 100-hectare farm and must decide how many hectares to allocate to three crops $C_1, C_2, C_3$. The profit per hectare is ₹4000, ₹5000, and ₹3000 respectively. To ensure crop rotation, at least 20 hectares must be of $C_1$, at most 30 hectares of $C_2$, and the area of $C_3$ must be at least the area of $C_1$. Formulate as an LPP and classify.

### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Decision Variables [1 mark]:**
Let $x_1, x_2, x_3$ = hectares allocated to crops $C_1, C_2, C_3$.

**Step 2 — Objective Function [1.5 marks]:**
Total profit $P$ in rupees:
$$P = 4000 x_1 + 5000 x_2 + 3000 x_3$$
Goal: **Maximize** $P$.

**Step 3 — Constraints [2.5 marks]:**
Total land (at most 100 ha):
$$x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \le 100$$
Crop rotation (at least 20 ha of $C_1$):
$$x_1 \ge 20$$
Crop rotation (at most 30 ha of $C_2$):
$$x_2 \le 30$$
Crop rotation ($C_3 \ge C_1$):
$$x_3 - x_1 \ge 0$$

**Step 4 — Non-negativity [1 mark]:**
$$x_1, x_2, x_3 \ge 0$$

**Step 5 — Standard Form [1 mark]:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Maximize } \quad & P = 4000 x_1 + 5000 x_2 + 3000 x_3 \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \le 100 \\
& x_1 \ge 20 \\
& x_2 \le 30 \\
& x_3 - x_1 \ge 0 \\
& x_1, x_2, x_3 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Classification:** **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** with 3 variables and 4 functional constraints.

---

> **Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** A dairy produces two milk products, toned milk and double-toned milk. Each litre of toned milk uses 0.5 kg milk solids; each litre of double-toned milk uses 0.4 kg milk solids. The plant has 1000 kg of milk solids per day. Production capacity is 2500 L/day for toned and 2000 L/day for double-toned. Profit is ₹20/L and ₹15/L respectively. The government requires at least 3000 L of toned milk daily for the public distribution system. Formulate as a Mathematical Programming Problem and identify the four components.

### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Decision Variables [1 mark]:**
$x_1$ = litres of toned milk produced per day.
$x_2$ = litres of double-toned milk produced per day.

**Step 2 — Objective Function [1.5 marks]:**
Total daily profit $Z$ in rupees:
$$Z = 20 x_1 + 15 x_2$$
Goal: **Maximize** $Z$.

**Step 3 — Constraints [2.5 marks]:**
Milk solids limit (at most 1000 kg/day):
$$0.5 x_1 + 0.4 x_2 \le 1000$$
Toned milk production capacity:
$$x_1 \le 2500$$
Double-toned milk production capacity:
$$x_2 \le 2000$$
Government PDS requirement (at least 3000 L of toned):
$$x_1 \ge 3000$$

**Step 4 — Non-negativity [1 mark]:**
$$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

**Step 5 — Standard Form [1 mark]:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Maximize } \quad & Z = 20 x_1 + 15 x_2 \\
\textbf{Subject to } \quad & 0.5 x_1 + 0.4 x_2 \le 1000 \\
& x_1 \le 2500 \\
& x_2 \le 2000 \\
& x_1 \ge 3000 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Four Components Identification [Bonus 0 marks — covered implicitly]:**
- **Decision Variables:** $x_1, x_2$ (litres/day of each milk type).
- **Objective Function:** $Z = 20 x_1 + 15 x_2$ (profit to be maximized).
- **Constraints:** milk solids, capacity, PDS requirement.
- **Parameters:** $0.5, 0.4, 1000, 2500, 2000, 3000$ (fixed given constants).

**Classification:** **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Formulation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Non-Negativity Restriction ($x_i \ge 0$):** This alone costs **1 mark** in most 14-mark questions. Always write it explicitly, even if it "seems obvious."
>
> 2. **Confusing the Direction of Inequalities:** A problem saying *"at most 100 hours"* gives $\le$; *"at least 20 units"* gives $\ge$. Misreading costs **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Missing the Objective Function Sense:** Writing Minimize when the problem says "maximize profit" loses the optimization direction. The KTU board checks this *first*.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Units in Decision Variables:** Always state $x_1 =$ *"number of units"* or *"kg"* or *"hectares"* — not just "$x_1$". A bare variable name without physical units loses 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 5. **Failing to Classify the MPP:** A full-mark formulation *must* end with the classification line — **LPP, NLPP, IPP, QPP, GPP** — with one-line justification (linearity / nonlinearity / integrality).
>
> 6. **Over-Complicating:** Do not introduce unnecessary variables. If only two products exist, do *not* invent a third.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Formulation of MPPs**

- ✅ A **Mathematical Programming Problem (MPP)** is a formal model: $\min/\max f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le b_j$ and $x_i \ge 0$.
- ✅ Every MPP has **exactly four components**: Decision Variables, Objective Function, Constraints, Parameters.
- ✅ **Decision variables** are the *controllable* quantities the designer chooses; **parameters** are fixed given constants.
- ✅ The **objective function** is a *scalar* — not a vector — and is always **minimized** or **maximized**.
- ✅ Standard canonical forms: (I) Min + $\le$; (II) Max + $\ge$. Convert as needed by multiplying inequalities by $-1$.
- ✅ **LPP** ↔ all $f$ and $g_j$ are linear. **NLPP** ↔ at least one is nonlinear. **IPP** ↔ integrality of variables. **QPP** ↔ quadratic $f$ with linear $g_j$. **GPP** ↔ posynomial form.
- ✅ Always include **non-negativity** $x_i \ge 0$ unless a sign is explicitly given.
- ✅ Use the **six-step template** (Variables → Objective → Constraints → Bounds → Combine → Classify) in every formulation answer.
- ✅ The phrase *"at most"* → $\le$; *"at least"* → $\ge$; *"equal to"* → $=$.
- ✅ In engineering design (beam, spring, vessel, truss), the objective is usually *minimize weight/volume* and the constraints are *stress, deflection, geometry*.
- ✅ The classification line is **mandatory** at the end of every formulation.
- ✅ A product of two decision variables ($x_1 \cdot x_2$) makes the function **nonlinear** even if each appears to a unit power.
- ✅ A product term $r h$ in the objective can sometimes be *substituted* (e.g., $y = rh$) to **linearize** the problem — a frequent KTU trick.
- ✅ The **feasible region** $S = \{\mathbf{x} : g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le b_j,\, x_i \ge 0\}$ is the set of all admissible designs.
- ✅ The **optimal solution** $\mathbf{x}^{\star}$ is the point in $S$ that minimizes (or maximizes) $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- ✅ Formulation is a **modeling art** — the same real problem can yield different valid mathematical forms depending on the choice of decision variables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Optimization Problems and Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET755 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Optimization Problems and Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Optimization?

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon, **Optimization** is the mathematical discipline of selecting the *best* element (with respect to some criterion) from a set of available alternatives. It involves finding the **maximum or minimum** value of an objective function subject to a set of constraints imposed on the decision variables.

Formally, a general optimization problem is stated as:

$$\text{Find } \mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$$

$$\text{that minimizes (or maximizes) } f(\mathbf{x})$$

$$\text{subject to: } g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

$$h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the **decision variable vector**, $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**, $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ are **inequality constraints**, and $h_k(\mathbf{x})$ are **equality constraints**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Optimization techniques are mathematical procedures used to find the most efficient solution to a problem, where efficiency is measured by an objective function and governed by constraints.

## 1.2 Why Classify Optimization Problems?

Just like a doctor needs to classify a disease before prescribing medicine, an engineer must classify an optimization problem before selecting an appropriate solution technique. **Linear Programming (LP)** cannot be solved by the same method as **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)**; **Integer Programming** requires different tools than **Continuous Optimization**.

> [!NOTE]
> The classification framework in this module acts as a **decision tree** that guides the engineer toward the most suitable algorithmic family for a given engineering problem.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Traveller's Optimization Problem

Imagine you are planning a road trip from Kochi to Delhi. You have several **decisions** to make:

- **Decision Variables:** Route choice, fuel type, stops, departure time.
- **Objective Function:** Minimise total travel time (or cost, or fuel).
- **Constraints:** Budget limit, vehicle range, delivery deadline.

Now, suppose:
- If the route can be any real distance (continuous), it's a **continuous** problem.
- If you must choose between exactly 3 highways (discrete), it's a **discrete/combinatorial** problem.
- If only fuel cost matters, it's a **single-objective** problem.
- If you care about both time AND fuel AND comfort, it's a **multi-objective** problem.

This real-world scenario mirrors the engineering optimization landscape perfectly.

## 1.4 Engineering Applications

| Domain | Optimization Use Case |
|--------|----------------------|
| Mechanical Design | Minimise weight of a beam subject to stress limits |
| Electrical Systems | Optimal power flow in a grid |
| Civil Engineering | Minimum-cost structural design |
| Manufacturing | Optimal cutting patterns to minimise waste |
| Logistics | Vehicle routing and scheduling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Optimization Problems

Optimization problems are classified along **multiple independent axes**. A single real-world problem may simultaneously belong to several categories (e.g., a *nonlinear*, *constrained*, *multi-objective*, *deterministic* problem).

### 2.1.1 Based on the Nature of the Objective Function and Constraints

#### A. Linear Programming (LP)

Both the objective function and all constraints are **linear** in the decision variables.

**Standard Form:**

$$\text{Minimise } f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$$

$$\text{subject to } \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b}, \quad \mathbf{x} \ge 0$$

**Solution Techniques:** Simplex Method, Graphical Method, Dual Simplex, Interior Point Method.

#### B. Nonlinear Programming (NLP)

At least one of the objective function or constraints is **nonlinear**.

**Standard Form:**

$$\text{Minimise } f(\mathbf{x})$$

$$\text{subject to } g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

$$h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

**Solution Techniques:** Gradient descent, Newton's method, KKT conditions, Penalty methods, Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP).

#### C. Quadratic Programming (QP)

A special case of NLP where the objective function is **quadratic** and constraints are **linear**.

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}$$

### 2.1.2 Based on the Nature of Decision Variables

- **Continuous Variable Optimization:** Variables can take any real value within a range (e.g., $x \in [0, 10]$).
- **Discrete / Integer Programming (IP):** Variables are restricted to integer or discrete values.
- **Mixed Integer Programming (MIP):** Some variables are integer, others continuous.
- **Combinatorial Optimization:** Finite but extremely large set of alternatives (e.g., Travelling Salesman Problem - TSP).

### 2.1.3 Based on the Existence of Constraints

- **Unconstrained Optimization:** No restrictions on variables other than the domain.
- **Constrained Optimization:** Subject to equality and/or inequality constraints.

### 2.1.4 Based on the Number of Objective Functions

- **Single-Objective Optimization:** One objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Multi-Objective Optimization:** Multiple conflicting objectives $F(\mathbf{x}) = [f_1, f_2, \dots, f_k]$; solved via **Pareto optimality**.

### 2.1.5 Based on the Nature of the Problem Environment

- **Static (Steady-State) Optimization:** Time-invariant; parameters do not change with time.
- **Dynamic Optimization:** Time-varying; involves differential equations (optimal control).

### 2.1.6 Based on Determinism of Parameters

- **Deterministic Optimization:** All parameters are known with certainty.
- **Stochastic Optimization:** Some parameters are random variables with known probability distributions.

### 2.1.7 Based on Convexity

- **Convex Programming:** Objective is convex (minimisation) and feasible region is a convex set — guarantees global optimum.
- **Non-Convex Programming:** Multiple local optima possible; global solution is hard.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Convexity is the single most important property distinguishing easy problems from hard ones. A convex problem guarantees that any local minimum is a global minimum.

## 2.2 Classification of Optimization Techniques

The solution techniques themselves are classified into two broad families:

### 2.2.1 Classical (Indirect) Methods

- Use **differential calculus** to locate stationary points.
- Rely on the necessary condition: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ (for unconstrained).
- Used when the function is **continuous and differentiable**.
- Examples: Lagrange Multipliers, Kuhn-Tucker conditions, Jacobian & Hessian analysis.

### 2.2.2 Numerical (Direct / Iterative / Search) Methods

- Used when classical calculus fails (non-differentiable, discrete, multimodal).
- Generate a sequence of iterates $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ that approach the optimum.
- Examples: Simplex Method, Gradient Descent, Newton's Method, Genetic Algorithm (GA), Simulated Annealing.

### 2.2.3 Modern / Metaheuristic Methods

- Inspired by natural phenomena; handle non-convex, non-differentiable problems.
- Examples: Genetic Algorithms (GA), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Condition |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Necessary Condition (Unconstrained) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Stationary point |
| Second-Order Sufficient Condition | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) > 0$ (PD matrix) | Local minimum |
| Lagrangian Function | $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \mu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$ | Constrained problems |
| KKT Stationarity | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L = 0$ | All multipliers $\ge 0$ |
| Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | At optimality |
| LP Standard Form | Min $\mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}$ s.t. $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{x} \ge 0$ |
| Convexity Test | $f(\theta x_1 + (1-\theta) x_2) \le \theta f(x_1) + (1-\theta) f(x_2)$ | For all $\theta \in [0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In aerospace engineering, trajectory optimisation (a dynamic, nonlinear, constrained problem) uses the same KKT framework described here, scaled up with hundreds of variables. The classification you learn today drives every major engineering decision tomorrow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Necessary Condition (Unconstrained)

We want to derive the condition under which a point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a local minimum of a differentiable function $f(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 1: Taylor Series Expansion**
Expand $f(\mathbf{x})$ in the neighbourhood of $\mathbf{x}^*$ along a small perturbation $\delta \mathbf{x}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \delta\mathbf{x}) &= f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \delta\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2} \delta\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \delta\mathbf{x} + \mathcal{O}(\vert\vert \delta\mathbf{x} \vert\vert^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Condition for Local Minimum**
For $\mathbf{x}^*$ to be a local minimum, the function value at nearby points must be **greater than or equal to** $f(\mathbf{x}^*)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \delta\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) &\ge 0 \quad \text{for small } \delta\mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: First-Order Term Must Vanish**
The linear term $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \delta\mathbf{x}$ can be made positive or negative by the choice of sign of $\delta\mathbf{x}$, unless its coefficient is zero.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \delta\mathbf{x} &= 0 \quad \text{for all } \delta\mathbf{x} \\
\therefore \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) &= \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Second-Order Sufficient Condition**
Substituting the first-order condition back, we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \delta\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) &\approx \frac{1}{2} \delta\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \delta\mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

For this to be non-negative for all $\delta\mathbf{x} \ne \mathbf{0}$, the Hessian matrix $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ must be **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)**. For a *strict* local minimum, $\mathbf{H}$ must be **Positive Definite (PD)**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lagrangian Formulation (Constrained)

**Step 1: Combine Objective and Constraints**
Introduce a scalar multiplier $\lambda_i$ for each inequality constraint $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ and $\mu_j$ for each equality constraint $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) &= f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \, h_j(\mathbf{x})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: KKT Stationarity Condition**
Taking the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ and setting it to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L &= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Primal Feasibility**
The optimal point must satisfy all constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(\mathbf{x}) &\le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
h_j(\mathbf{x}) &= 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Dual Feasibility**
For inequality constraints, multipliers must be non-negative:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_i &\ge 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Complementary Slackness**
At optimality, either the constraint is *active* ($g_i = 0$) or its multiplier is zero ($\lambda_i = 0$) — never both violated.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) &= 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Problem Type → Solution Method

| Problem Classification | Problem Signature | Recommended Technique | Method Type |
|------------------------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| LP, Continuous | Linear $f$, linear $g$, linear $h$ | Simplex Method | Classical/Numerical |
| LP, Equality Constraints | LP with $h(\mathbf{x})=0$ | Dual Simplex | Numerical |
| NLP, Unconstrained | Nonlinear $f$, no constraints | Steepest Descent / Newton | Numerical |
| NLP, Constrained | Nonlinear $f$, nonlinear $g$ | KKT Conditions + Penalty | Classical |
| QP, Linear Constraints | Quadratic $f$, linear $g$ | Wolfe's Method | Numerical |
| Integer Programming | $x \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ | Branch & Bound | Combinatorial |
| Dynamic Optimisation | $\dot{x} = f(x,u,t)$ | Pontryagin's Principle | Classical |
| Multi-Objective | Multiple $f_i(\mathbf{x})$ | Weighted Sum, $\epsilon$-constraint | Metaheuristic |
| Stochastic | Random parameters | Stochastic Programming | Modern |
| Non-Convex / Multimodal | Multiple local minima | GA, PSO, Simulated Annealing | Metaheuristic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Problem] --> B{Decision Variables Type}
    B -- Real Values --> C[Continuous]
    B -- Whole Numbers --> D[Integer IP]
    B -- Mixed --> E[Mixed Integer MIP]
    D --> F{Combinatorial Explosion}
    F -- Yes --> G[Combinatorial Optimization]
    F -- No --> H[Branch and Bound]

    A --> I{Objective and Constraint Type}
    I -- All Linear --> J[Linear Programming LP]
    I -- Quadratic Objective --> K[Quadratic Programming QP]
    I -- Any Nonlinear --> L[Nonlinear Programming NLP]

    A --> M{Number of Objectives}
    M -- Single --> N[Single Objective]
    M -- Multiple --> O[Multi Objective Pareto]

    A --> P{Parameter Certainty}
    P -- Known --> Q[Deterministic]
    P -- Random --> R[Stochastic]

    A --> S{Time Dependence}
    S -- Time Invariant --> T[Static Programming]
    S -- Time Varying --> U[Dynamic Programming]

    J --> V[Simplex Method]
    L --> W[KKT Conditions]
    L --> X[Penalty Methods]
    O --> Y[Weighted Sum Method]
    U --> Z[Pontryagin Maximum Principle]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solution Methodology Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real World Engineering Problem] --> B[Mathematical Formulation]
    B --> C{Identify Objective Function}
    B --> D{Identify Constraints}
    B --> E{Identify Variables}

    C --> F{Linear or Nonlinear}
    D --> F
    E --> G{Continuous or Discrete}

    F -- Linear --> H[LP Solver Simplex]
    F -- Quadratic --> I[QP Solver Wolfe]
    F -- Nonlinear --> J[NLP Solver KKT Penalty]

    G -- Continuous --> H
    G -- Discrete --> K[IP Solver Branch Bound]
    G -- Mixed --> L[MIP Solver]

    J --> M[Verify Convexity]
    M -- Convex --> N[Guaranteed Global Optimum]
    M -- Non Convex --> O[Metaheuristics GA PSO]

    H --> P[Optimal Solution]
    I --> P
    J --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.3 Algorithm Family Tree (Techniques Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Techniques] --> B[Classical Methods]
    A --> C[Numerical Methods]
    A --> D[Metaheuristic Methods]

    B --> B1[Calculus Based]
    B --> B2[Variational Methods]
    B1 --> B1a[Lagrange Multipliers]
    B1 --> B1b[KKT Conditions]
    B2 --> B2a[Pontryagin Principle]

    C --> C1[Direct Search]
    C --> C2[Gradient Based]
    C --> C3[Linear Algebraic]
    C1 --> C1a[Hooke Jeeves]
    C1 --> C1b[Nelder Mead Simplex]
    C2 --> C2a[Steepest Descent]
    C2 --> C2b[Newton Raphson]
    C2 --> C2c[Conjugate Gradient]
    C3 --> C3a[Simplex Method]
    C3 --> C3b[Interior Point]

    D --> D1[Evolutionary]
    D --> D2[Swarm Intelligence]
    D --> D3[Physical Process Based]
    D1 --> D1a[Genetic Algorithm GA]
    D1 --> D1b[Differential Evolution]
    D2 --> D2a[Particle Swarm PSO]
    D2 --> D2b[Ant Colony ACO]
    D3 --> D3a[Simulated Annealing]
    D3 --> D3b[Tabu Search]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Differentiate between Linear Programming (LP) and Nonlinear Programming (NLP) problems. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Linear Programming (LP) | Nonlinear Programming (NLP) |
|---------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Objective Function | Linear in variables | Nonlinear (quadratic, cubic, exponential, etc.) |
| Constraints | All linear | At least one nonlinear |
| Solution Method | Simplex Method | KKT, Gradient Methods |
| Global Optimum | Guaranteed (convex) | Not guaranteed (non-convex possible) |
| Example | Product mix with linear profit & resource constraints | Minimise $f(x) = x_3 - 2x$ subject to $x_2 \ge x^2$ |

**Example (LP):** Minimise cost $= 4x_1 + 3x_2$ subject to $2x_1 + x_2 \ge 10, \; x_1 + 3x_2 \ge 12, \; x_1, x_2 \ge 0$.

**Example (NLP):** Minimise $f(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 = 1$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: What are the necessary and sufficient conditions for a point to be a local minimum of an unconstrained function $f(\mathbf{x})$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Necessary Condition:** If $f(\mathbf{x})$ is differentiable and $\mathbf{x}^*$ is an unconstrained local minimum, then:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

That is, the gradient vector (first partial derivatives with respect to all $n$ variables) must vanish at $\mathbf{x}^*$.

**Sufficient Condition:** If $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and the Hessian matrix $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **Positive Definite** (i.e., all eigenvalues $> 0$), then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.

> If $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is only Positive Semi-Definite, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a local minimum (not necessarily strict), and higher-order terms must be examined.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For Part B questions on classification, students frequently lose marks by (a) failing to provide examples for each class, (b) not mentioning the corresponding solution method, and (c) writing only definitions without the structural comparison table. Always include **at least one example** per category and **map each problem type to its solution technique**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Classify optimization problems based on (i) nature of variables, (ii) nature of the objective function and constraints, (iii) number of objectives, and (iv) determinism of parameters. Give one engineering example for each category.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Based on Nature of Variables:**

- **Continuous Variable Optimization:** Variables can assume any real value within bounds. *Example:* Finding the optimal diameter $d \in [10, 50]$ mm of a shaft to minimise material cost.
- **Discrete / Integer Programming:** Variables restricted to integer values. *Example:* Determining how many buses ($n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$) to deploy in a city transit system.
- **Mixed Integer Programming:** Combination of both. *Example:* Optimal power generation where generator on/off is binary but power output is continuous.
- **Combinatorial Optimization:** Discrete variables with exponential alternatives. *Example:* Travelling Salesman Problem visiting 20 cities.

**(ii) Based on Nature of Objective & Constraints:**

- **Linear Programming:** *Example:* Product mix problem in a factory to maximise profit under linear resource constraints.
- **Nonlinear Programming:** *Example:* Minimising the weight of a truss structure $f(x) = \sum \rho_i A_i L_i$ subject to nonlinear stress constraints.
- **Quadratic Programming:** *Example:* Portfolio optimisation minimising variance (a quadratic objective) subject to linear budget constraints.

**(iii) Based on Number of Objectives:**

- **Single Objective:** *Example:* Minimise the cost of a single product.
- **Multi-Objective:** *Example:* Vehicle design where weight, cost, and emissions must be simultaneously minimised — leads to a **Pareto frontier**.

**(iv) Based on Determinism:**

- **Deterministic:** *Example:* Cutting-stock problem with known material dimensions.
- **Stochastic:** *Example:* Inventory optimisation where demand is a random variable with known probability distribution.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions for a constrained nonlinear optimisation problem. State the four conditions clearly and explain their physical meaning.**

**Model Solution:**

The KKT conditions are the **first-order necessary conditions** for optimality in a constrained NLP problem with both inequality and equality constraints.

**General Problem:**

Minimise $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ ($i = 1, \dots, m$) and $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ ($j = 1, \dots, p$).

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian:**

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2 — State the Four KKT Conditions:**

**Condition 1 — Stationarity (Gradient of L vanishes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Meaning:* The objective function gradient must be balanced by the weighted sum of constraint gradients.

**Condition 2 — Primal Feasibility:**

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

*Meaning:* The optimal solution must lie inside the feasible region (all inequality constraints respected, equalities satisfied).

**Condition 3 — Dual Feasibility:**

$$\lambda_i \ge 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, m$$

*Meaning:* Multipliers for inequality constraints cannot be negative (they represent the "shadow price" or marginal cost of constraint violation).

**Condition 4 — Complementary Slackness:**

$$\lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

*Meaning:* A constraint is either *inactive* (slack, $\lambda_i = 0$) or *active* ($g_i = 0$ with positive multiplier). Both cannot be strictly non-zero simultaneously.

**Physical Interpretation:** The multiplier $\lambda_i$ represents the rate of change of the optimal objective value per unit relaxation of the $i$-th constraint — i.e., the **marginal value of relaxing the constraint**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks] With a clear block diagram, explain the classification of optimization techniques. Compare classical methods with numerical/metaheuristic methods.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

Optimization Techniques are classified into three major families:

```
OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
│
├── 1. CLASSICAL METHODS (Indirect / Calculus-Based)
│       ├── Lagrange Multiplier Method
│       ├── Kuhn-Tucker Conditions (Generalised Lagrange)
│       ├── Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (Dynamic Systems)
│       └── Variational Methods
│
├── 2. NUMERICAL METHODS (Direct / Iterative)
│       ├── Linear: Simplex, Interior Point
│       ├── Gradient-Based: Steepest Descent, Newton-Raphson, Conjugate Gradient
│       └── Direct Search: Hooke-Jeeves, Nelder-Mead
│
└── 3. METAHEURISTIC / MODERN METHODS
        ├── Evolutionary: Genetic Algorithm (GA), Differential Evolution
        ├── Swarm Intelligence: PSO, ACO
        └── Physical Process: Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Classical Methods | Numerical Methods | Metaheuristic Methods |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Foundation | Differential Calculus | Iterative Computation | Natural Phenomena Mimicry |
| Gradient Required | Yes (always) | Usually yes (some no) | No |
| Handles Discontinuity | No | Limited | Yes |
