> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in $N$**: Many students write $N = (b-a)/\Delta$, missing the inclusive count. This causes off-by-one errors.
> 2. **Mixing up $\Delta x$ formula**: Resolution is $(b-a)/(2^L - 1)$, NOT $(b-a)/2^L$. The denominator is $2^L - 1$ because there are $2^L - 1$ intervals between $2^L$ points.
> 3. **Wrong decoding order**: Bits must be read from LSB (rightmost) to MSB (leftmost). Reversing gives wrong values.
> 4. **Using standard crossover with permutation encoding**: Examiner deducts 2 marks for not mentioning PMX/OX.
> 5. **Concatenation errors**: When concatenating multi-variable chromosomes, students forget to track which bit substring belongs to which variable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Encoding = Phenotype-to-Genotype Mapping.** It is the first and most critical design decision in any GA.
- **Five main encoding schemes**: Binary, Real-valued, Integer, Permutation, Tree.
- **Decoding equation for binary continuous mapping**:
  $$x = a + \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1} \cdot \text{decimal}(b_{L-1} \dots b_0)$$
- **Resolution formula**: $\Delta x = (b-a)/(2^L - 1)$
- **Minimum bit length**: $L = \lceil \log_2((b-a)/\Delta x + 1) \rceil$
- **Binary encoding** suits discretized continuous problems; **Real-valued** suits pure continuous optimization; **Permutation** suits ordering problems; **Tree** suits symbolic regression/GP.
- **Permutation encoding requires specialized operators** (PMX, OX, Inversion) — never use standard crossover.
- **Multi-variable problems** use *concatenated chromosomes* with separate bit-length allocations per variable.
- **Encoding-Decoding consistency** is mandatory; mismatches produce invalid solutions.
- **Higher bit length $L$** = finer resolution but slower convergence (larger search space).
- **Real-valued encoding** is preferred in modern GAs and Evolution Strategies for high-dimensional continuous problems.
- **Remember the +1** in $N$ calculation and the **−1** in the $2^L - 1$ denominator — these are the two most common KTU marking traps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mutation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET755 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mutation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Genetic Algorithms (GAs)** and **Evolutionary Computation**, **Mutation** is defined as a *background genetic operator* that introduces **small, stochastic perturbations** into the genetic structure (chromosome) of an offspring individual, with the primary objective of **maintaining genetic diversity** within the population and **preventing premature convergence** to local optima.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET755 – Module 4):**
> Mutation is a unary genetic operator that alters one or more gene values in a chromosome from their initial state, governed by a **mutation probability** (denoted as $p_m$ or $\mu$). It acts as a *secondary exploration mechanism* in the GA pipeline, complementing the *exploitation* performed by crossover.

Mathematically, a mutation operator is a stochastic mapping:

$$M : \mathbb{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{S}$$

where $\mathbb{S}$ is the **search space (solution space)** of candidate solutions. The application of $M$ on a parent chromosome $P$ produces an offspring $P'$ with the property:

$$P' = P \oplus \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Distribution}(p_m)$$

where $\eta$ is a random perturbation vector drawn from a distribution parameterized by the **mutation rate $p_m$**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Spice in a Recipe"**
> Imagine a master chef who has perfected a recipe over many generations (crossover = mixing good recipes from parent chefs). To occasionally discover a *brand-new flavor*, the chef adds a **pinch of an unexpected spice** to one dish in every hundred. Most of the time, this random addition spoils the dish. **Rarely, it produces a culinary masterpiece.**
> - The **recipe** = the chromosome
> - The **pinch of spice** = a single mutation event
> - The **frequency of adding spice** = mutation probability $p_m$
> - The **rare masterpiece** = a beneficial mutation that escapes a local optimum

A second analogy: think of mutation as the **biological process of copying errors during DNA replication**. In nature, these random errors occasionally produce advantageous traits (e.g., lactose tolerance in adults). In a GA, mutation analogously allows the algorithm to **reach parts of the search space that crossover alone cannot access**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutation Probability | $p_m$ | $0.001$ to $0.1$ | Controls frequency of gene alteration |
| Chromosome Length | $L$ | Problem-specific | Number of genes in chromosome |
| Expected Mutated Genes | $E[K]$ | $L \cdot p_m$ | Mean number of mutations per offspring |
| Standard Deviation (Real-coded) | $\sigma$ | $(0.1 \text{ to } 0.3) \cdot \text{range}$ | Step size for Gaussian perturbation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mutation Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> Without mutation, GAs suffer from **premature convergence** — the entire population collapses to identical (or near-identical) chromosomes, and the algorithm loses its ability to explore. Mutation is the **"escape hatch"** from local optima.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Role of Mutation in the GA Pipeline

The standard **Genetic Algorithm cycle** is:

**Selection → Crossover → Mutation → Replacement → New Generation**

Mutation sits at the **post-crossover** stage. Its position in the pipeline is critical:

- **Crossover** performs **exploitation** (refines good solutions by recombining building blocks)
- **Mutation** performs **exploration** (introduces novel genetic material)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exploration vs Exploitation Trade-off:**
> If $p_m$ is too **low** (e.g., $10^{-6}$), mutation is effectively inactive → population stagnates.
> If $p_m$ is too **high** (e.g., $0.5$), the algorithm becomes a **random search** → good solutions are destroyed as fast as they are created.
> The classical **De Jong (1975)** recommendation is $p_m \in [0.005, 0.01]$ for binary GAs.

### 2.2 Classification of Mutation Operators

#### 2.2.1 For Binary / Discrete Chromosomes

| Operator | Mechanism | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit-Flip Mutation** | Each bit flipped with prob $p_m$: $0 \leftrightarrow 1$ | Binary GAs, bit-string problems |
| **Swap Mutation** | Two randomly chosen positions have values exchanged | Permutation problems (TSP) |
| **Inversion Mutation** | A sub-string is reversed in place | Permutation problems |
| **Scramble Mutation** | A sub-string is randomly shuffled | Combinatorial optimization |

#### 2.2.2 For Real-Coded (Continuous) Chromosomes

| Operator | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Mutation** | $x_i' = x_i + r \cdot (u_i - l_i)$, $r \in [0,1]$ | Simple, may overshoot |
| **Gaussian Mutation** | $x_i' = x_i + N(0, \sigma)$ | Smooth perturbation |
| **Polynomial Mutation (Deb)** | Uses polynomial probability distribution | Bounded, preferred in NSGA-II |
| **Non-Uniform Mutation** | $x_i' = x_i + \Delta(t, u_i - x_i)$ or $-\Delta(t, x_i - l_i)$ | Time-dependent step size |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Must memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E[K] = L \cdot p_m$ | Expected number of mutated genes in a chromosome of length $L$ |
| 2 | $P(K = k) = \binom{L}{k} p_m^k (1 - p_m)^{L-k}$ | Probability of exactly $k$ mutations (Binomial) |
| 3 | $P(\text{no mutation}) = (1 - p_m)^L$ | Probability a chromosome is unchanged |
| 4 | $x_i' = 1 - x_i$ (if selected) | Bit-flip mutation rule |
| 5 | $x_i' = x_i + N(0, \sigma^2)$ | Gaussian mutation (real-coded) |
| 6 | $x_i' = x_i + \delta \cdot (u_i - l_i)$, $\delta \in [-\alpha, \alpha]$ | Uniform mutation (real-coded) |
| 7 | $\sigma' = \sigma \cdot e^{\tau \cdot N(0,1) + \tau' \cdot N_i(0,1)}$ | Self-adaptive mutation (Evolution Strategies) |
| 8 | $\Delta(t, y) = y \cdot \left(1 - r^{(1-t/T)^b}\right)$ | Non-uniform mutation step (Michalewicz) |

where in (8): $r \sim U(0,1)$, $t$ = current generation, $T$ = max generations, $b$ = non-uniformity degree (often $b=5$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Applications of Mutation-Based GAs in Engineering:**
> - **Aerospace:** Optimal wing-shape design, trajectory optimization
> - **VLSI:** Placement and routing of circuit components
> - **Power Systems:** Optimal economic dispatch of generators
> - **Robotics:** Evolving walking gaits for legged robots
> - **Bioinformatics:** Protein structure prediction, RNA folding

Mutation is the **engine of novelty** in all these domains — without it, the search becomes repetitive and gets trapped in local optima corresponding to suboptimal engineering designs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Expected Number of Mutated Genes

**Statement:** For a chromosome of length $L$ with per-gene mutation probability $p_m$, derive the expected number of mutated genes $E[K]$.

**Step 1 — Define indicator random variables.**
For each gene $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L\}$, define:

$$X_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if gene } i \text{ is mutated} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Total number of mutations is the sum:**

$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{L} X_i$$

**Step 3 — Take the expectation, using linearity:**

$$E[K] = E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{L} X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{L} E[X_i]$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E[X_i]$.** Since $X_i$ is a Bernoulli random variable with success probability $p_m$:

$$E[X_i] = 1 \cdot P(X_i = 1) + 0 \cdot P(X_i = 0) = p_m$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$E[K] = \sum_{i=1}^{L} p_m = L \cdot p_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{E[K] = L \cdot p_m}$

### 3.2 Derivation: Probability of a Chromosome Being Unchanged

**Step 1 — A chromosome is unchanged only if *all* $L$ genes escape mutation.**
**Step 2 — Genes mutate independently with probability $p_m$ each.**
**Step 3 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent events:**

$$P(\text{no mutation}) = \prod_{i=1}^{L} (1 - p_m) = (1 - p_m)^L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** For $L = 20$ bits and $p_m = 0.01$, we get $(0.99)^{20} \approx 0.818$. So ~82% of offspring pass through mutation unchanged — confirming that mutation is a **gentle** operator, not a destructive one.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Bit-Flip Mutation on a Binary Chromosome

**Problem:** A binary chromosome $\mathbf{x} = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0]$ of length $L = 8$ is subjected to bit-flip mutation with $p_m = 0.25$. Show the result of one mutation cycle.

**Step 1 — Generate a uniform random number $r_i \in [0,1)$ for each gene $i = 1, \ldots, 8$.**

Suppose the random numbers generated are:

| Gene $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Original $x_i$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $r_i$ | 0.18 | 0.42 | 0.31 | 0.09 | 0.67 | 0.55 | 0.21 | 0.84 |
| Mutate? ($r_i < 0.25$?) | Yes | No | No | Yes | No | No | Yes | No |

**Step 2 — Apply flip rule $x_i' = 1 - x_i$ for the selected genes (1, 4, 7).**

$$x_1' = 1 - 1 = 0$$
$$x_4' = 1 - 1 = 0$$
$$x_7' = 1 - 1 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Write the final offspring:**

$$\mathbf{x}' = [0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]$$

**Step 4 — Verification against expected count:**

$$E[K] = L \cdot p_m = 8 \cdot 0.25 = 2$$

We observed $K = 3$ mutations in this single trial, which is statistically reasonable (standard deviation of Binomial(8, 0.25) is $\sqrt{8 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.75} \approx 1.22$).

### 3.4 Worked Example: Gaussian Mutation (Real-Coded GA)

**Problem:** A real-coded chromosome $\mathbf{x} = [2.50, -1.30, 4.80]$ undergoes Gaussian mutation with $\sigma = 0.50$.

**Step 1 — Generate three independent samples from $N(0, 1)$:**

$$z_1 = 0.42, \quad z_2 = -1.15, \quad z_3 = 0.07$$

**Step 2 — Scale by $\sigma$:**

$$\eta_1 = 0.50 \cdot 0.42 = 0.210$$
$$\eta_2 = 0.50 \cdot (-1.15) = -0.575$$
$$\eta_3 = 0.50 \cdot 0.07 = 0.035$$

**Step 3 — Add perturbations to original genes:**

$$x_1' = 2.50 + 0.210 = 2.710$$
$$x_2' = -1.30 + (-0.575) = -1.875$$
$$x_3' = 4.80 + 0.035 = 4.835$$

$$\mathbf{x}' = [2.710, \; -1.875, \; 4.835]$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a real KTU exam, you may also need to apply **boundary clamping**: if $x_i'$ falls outside the feasible range $[l_i, u_i]$, reset it to the nearest bound. For example, if $u_2 = -1.5$, then $x_2' = -1.5$ (clamped).

### 3.5 Derivation: Self-Adaptive Mutation in Evolution Strategies

In **Evolution Strategies (ES)**, the mutation operator adapts its own step size $\sigma$ using the **log-normal rule**:

$$\sigma' = \sigma \cdot \exp(\tau \cdot N(0, 1) + \tau' \cdot N_i(0, 1))$$

where:
- $\tau \propto (2\sqrt{L})^{-1}$ (global learning rate)
- $\tau' \propto (\sqrt{L})^{-1}$ (coordinate-wise learning rate)
- $N_i(0,1)$ is sampled *fresh* for each coordinate

This is a **1/5-success rule** alternative: increase $\sigma$ when successful, decrease when not. It is widely tested in KTU questions on ES.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Position of Mutation in the Genetic Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Population P_t] --> B[Evaluate Fitness of each individual]
    B --> C[Selection: Roulette / Tournament / Rank]
    C --> D[Crossover: combine pairs to form offspring]
    D --> E{Mutation Probability p_m}
    E -- Yes --> F[Bit-flip / Gaussian / Swap mutation]
    E -- No --> G[Pass unchanged to next generation]
    F --> H[Evaluate offspring fitness]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Replacement: elitist / generational]
    I --> J{Convergence or t = T max?}
    J -- No --> B
    J -- Yes --> K[Output best solution]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Mutation Operator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Parent Chromosome] --> R[Random Number Generator]
    R --> D{R_i less than p_m}
    D -- True --> M[Mutate Gene i]
    D -- False --> U[Keep Gene i unchanged]
    M --> O[Offspring Chromosome]
    U --> O
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: Mutation Operator Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mutation Operators] --> B[Binary Encoding]
    M --> C[Real Encoding]
    M --> P[Permutation Encoding]
    B --> B1[Bit-Flip]
    B --> B2[Random Resetting]
    C --> C1[Gaussian]
    C --> C2[Uniform]
    C --> C3[Polynomial Deb]
    C --> C4[Non-Uniform Michalewicz]
    P --> P1[Swap]
    P --> P2[Inversion]
    P --> P3[Scramble]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow for Choosing a Mutation Operator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify Encoding] --> Q1{Encoding type?}
    Q1 -- Binary bits --> Q2{Use simple GA?}
    Q1 -- Real values --> Q3{Need bounded offspring?}
    Q1 -- Permutation --> Q4[Use Swap or Inversion]
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Bit-Flip with p_m approx 1 over L]
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Random Resetting]
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Polynomial or Non-Uniform]
    Q3 -- No --> R4[Gaussian with sigma tuning]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define mutation in Genetic Algorithms. Why is it considered a secondary operator compared to crossover?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Mutation is a unary genetic operator that introduces small, random alterations in one or more genes of a chromosome, governed by a mutation probability $p_m$. Formally, it is a stochastic mapping $M : \mathbb{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{S}$ that perturbs the offspring produced by crossover.
>
> **Why secondary (1.5 marks):** Mutation is applied **after** crossover and is invoked with a **low probability** ($p_m \approx 0.01$). Crossover is the **primary** search operator because it exploits building blocks from two fit parents, producing most of the improvement. Mutation acts as a **background** mechanism providing **genetic diversity** and **escape from local optima**, but if applied too frequently it degenerates the algorithm into random search.

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**A binary chromosome of length $L = 50$ is mutated with probability $p_m = 0.02$ per gene. Compute the expected number of mutated genes and the probability that the chromosome remains unchanged.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$E[K] = L \cdot p_m = 50 \cdot 0.02 = 1.0 \text{ gene}$$

$$P(\text{no mutation}) = (1 - p_m)^L = (0.98)^{50}$$

**Computation:**

$$\ln(0.98) = -0.02020, \quad 50 \cdot \ln(0.98) = -1.010$$

$$P(\text{no mutation}) = e^{-1.010} \approx 0.3642 \;\;(\approx 36.42\%)$$

> **Conclusion:** On average, **1 gene** is flipped per offspring, and there is a **~36% chance** the chromosome is passed through mutation unchanged.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model — 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the various types of mutation operators used in Genetic Algorithms for binary, real-coded, and permutation-based representations. Compare their suitability for different problem classes. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Binary Encoding (2 marks):**
- **Bit-Flip Mutation:** Each bit $x_i$ in a chromosome of length $L$ is flipped ($0 \to 1$ or $1 \to 0$) with probability $p_m$. Suitable for binary GAs solving knapsack, feature selection, or combinatorial subset problems.
- **Random Resetting:** Each bit is independently reset to 0 or 1 with probability $p_m$. Equivalent to bit-flip in expectation.

**Real-Coded Encoding (3 marks):**
- **Uniform Mutation:** $x_i' = x_i + r \cdot (u_i - l_i)$ where $r \sim U[0,1]$. Simple but may produce large jumps.
- **Gaussian Mutation:** $x_i' = x_i + N(0, \sigma^2)$. Produces smooth, normally distributed perturbations. Best for **continuous optimization** (e.g., Rosenbrock, Rastrigin functions).
- **Polynomial Mutation (Deb, 2001):** Generates bounded perturbations using a polynomial probability distribution. Preferred in **NSGA-II** for multi-objective problems.
- **Non-Uniform Mutation (Michalewicz):** Step size decreases with generation count, favoring fine-tuning in later stages.

**Permutation Encoding (2 marks):**
- **Swap Mutation:** Two positions $i, j$ are chosen uniformly at random and their values swapped.
- **Inversion Mutation:** A sub-string between two positions is reversed.
- **Scramble Mutation:** A sub-string is randomly shuffled.
- All three preserve the multiset of values — critical for **TSP, scheduling, assignment problems**.

**(b) For a binary chromosome of length $L = 100$ with mutation probability $p_m = 0.01$, derive the probability that exactly 2 genes are mutated. Also compute the expected number of mutated genes and the variance. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model the count of mutations $K$ as a Binomial random variable:**

$$K \sim \text{Binomial}(L, p_m) = \text{Binomial}(100, 0.01)$$

**Step 2 — Probability mass function:**

$$P(K = k) = \binom{L}{k} p_m^k (1 - p_m)^{L-k}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $L = 100$, $k = 2$, $p_m = 0.01$:**

$$P(K = 2) = \binom{100}{2} (0.01)^2 (0.99)^{98}$$

**Step 4 — Compute each component:**

$$\binom{100}{2} = \frac{100 \cdot 99}{2} = 4950$$

$$(0.01)^2 = 0.0001$$

$$\ln(0.99) = -0.01005, \quad 98 \cdot \ln(0.99) = -0.9849$$

$$(0.99)^{98} = e^{-0.9849} \approx 0.3735$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication:**

$$P(K = 2) = 4950 \cdot 0.0001 \cdot 0.3735 = 0.4950 \cdot 0.3735 \approx 0.1849$$

$$\boxed{P(K = 2) \approx 0.185 \;(18.5\%)}$$

**Step 6 — Expected number of mutations:**

$$E[K] = L \cdot p_m = 100 \cdot 0.01 = 1.0$$

**Step 7 — Variance of Binomial:**

$$\text{Var}(K) = L \cdot p_m \cdot (1 - p_m) = 100 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 0.99 = 0.99$$

$$\boxed{\text{Standard Deviation } \sigma_K = \sqrt{0.99} \approx 0.995}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mutation Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Some students write $L = 100$ as "100 bits" but forget to verify the bit-flip is **per gene** — mutation probability is **per gene**, not per chromosome.
> 2. **Formula misapplication:** The probability of *exactly* $k$ mutations uses the **Binomial PMF**, NOT a Poisson approximation (Poisson is an approximation valid only for $L$ large, $p_m$ small — examiners often deduct marks if you use Poisson without justification).
> 3. **Neglecting boundary clamping:** In real-coded mutation, students often forget to clamp offspring to feasible ranges. KTU examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for this.
> 4. **Confusing mutation with crossover:** Mutation is **unary** (one parent), crossover is **binary** (two parents). Examiners test this distinction.

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Alternative — 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the role of mutation probability $p_m$ in a Genetic Algorithm. Discuss the effect of choosing $p_m$ too high or too low, and state the recommended range. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of $p_m$ (3 marks):**
The mutation probability $p_m$ is the **single most important parameter** controlling the exploration behavior of a GA. It determines the **rate at which novel genetic material** is introduced into the population. A higher $p_m$ increases diversity but reduces the algorithm's ability to refine good solutions; a lower $p_m$ preserves good schemata but risks **premature convergence**.

**Effect of low $p_m$ (2 marks):**
- If $p_m \to 0$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$): The population becomes **homogeneous** after a few generations.
- The GA behaves like a **hill-climbing / crossover-only** algorithm.
- **Premature convergence** to a local optimum is highly likely.
- Recovery from loss of diversity is almost impossible without mutation.

**Effect of high $p_m$ (2 marks):**
- If $p_m \to 0.5$ or higher: Good solutions are constantly destroyed.
- The algorithm approximates **random search** or **random walk** in the search space.
- **Convergence to the global optimum is not guaranteed** because the building-block hypothesis fails.
- Convergence speed becomes extremely slow.

**Recommended range:** De Jong's classical recommendation is $p_m \in [0.005, 0.01]$ for binary GAs. For real-coded GAs, $p_m$ is often set to $1/L$, giving on average one mutation per offspring.

**(b) With reference to a real-coded chromosome $\mathbf{x} = [3.2, -2.1, 5.7]$ and Gaussian mutation with $\sigma = 0.5$, show the offspring produced if the random samples are $z_1 = 0.85$, $z_2 = -0.43$, $z_3 = 1.27$. Apply boundary clamping if the feasible region is $[-3, 5]$. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall Gaussian mutation rule:**

$$x_i' = x_i + \sigma \cdot z_i, \quad z_i \sim N(0,1)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the perturbation for each gene:**

$$\eta_1 = 0.5 \cdot 0.85 = 0.425$$
$$\eta_2 = 0.5 \cdot (-0.43) = -0.215$$
$$\eta_3 = 0.5 \cdot 1.27 = 0.635$$

**Step 3 — Compute raw offspring:**

$$x_1' = 3.2 + 0.425 = 3.625$$
$$x_2' = -2.1 + (-0.215) = -2.315$$
$$x_3' = 5.7 + 0.635 = 6.335$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary clamping to $[-3, 5]$:**

- $x_1' = 3.625$: Within $[-3, 5]$ → **keep as is**.
- $x_2' = -2.315$: Within $[-3, 5]$ → **keep as is**.
- $x_3' = 6.335$: **Exceeds upper bound** $5$ → **clamp to $5$**.

**Step 5 — Final offspring:**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}' = [3.625, \; -2.315, \; 5.000]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The clamping step (Step 4) is what distinguishes a **properly implemented** GA from a textbook one. KTU examiners specifically test this in Module 4 questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mutation in GAs**
>
> - [ ] **Definition:** Mutation is a **unary, stochastic** operator that flips/perturbs genes with probability $p_m$.
> - [ ] **Position in pipeline:** Comes **after crossover**, **before replacement**.
> - [ ] **Primary purpose:** Maintain **diversity**, **escape local optima**.
> - [ ] **Expected mutated genes:** $E[K] = L \cdot p_m$.
> - [ ] **Probability of no mutation:** $(1 - p_m)^L$.
> - [ ] **Probability of exactly $k$ mutations:** Binomial PMF, NOT Poisson (unless explicitly approximated).
> - [ ] **Recommended $p_m$ range:** $[0.005, 0.01]$ for binary; $1/L$ rule for both binary and real-coded.
> - [ ] **Real-coded mutation types:** Uniform, Gaussian, Polynomial, Non-Uniform — each with different step-size control.
> - [ ] **Permutation mutation types:** Swap, Inversion, Scramble — all preserve the value multiset.
> - [ ] **Always apply boundary clamping** in real-coded GAs.
> - [ ] **Mutation vs Crossover:** Mutation = exploration, Crossover = exploitation. Both are essential; one cannot substitute the other.
> - [ ] **Self-adaptive mutation** is used in Evolution Strategies (ES) with the log-normal rule.
> - [ ] **Key examiners' traps:** Per-gene vs per-chromosome probability; Binomial vs Poisson; boundary handling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Fuzzy Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET755 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Fuzzy Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classical (Crisp) Sets vs. Fuzzy Sets

In classical set theory (Aristotelian / Boolean logic), an element either **completely belongs** to a set or it does **not belong at all**. There is no intermediate state. This is the binary worldview enforced by the **Law of the Excluded Middle**: either $x \in A$ or $x \notin A$, never both.

Fuzzy Set Theory, introduced by **Lotfi A. Zadeh** in **1965**, generalizes this restriction by allowing elements to belong to a set with a **degree of membership** ranging anywhere on the closed interval $[0, 1]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Fuzzy Set* $\tilde{A}$ in a universe of discourse $X$ is characterized by a **membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}} : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$, where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ quantifies the grade (degree) to which element $x \in X$ belongs to $\tilde{A}$.

Formally, a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is written as:
$$\tilde{A} = \{ (x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X \}$$

For a discrete universe $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, the **canonical Zadeh's notation** is:

$$\tilde{A} = \frac{\mu_1}{x_1} + \frac{\mu_2}{x_2} + \cdots + \frac{\mu_n}{x_n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\mu_i}{x_i}$$

For a continuous universe:

$$\tilde{A} = \int_{X} \frac{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)}{x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $+$ and $\int$ signs here denote **aggregation of membership pairs**, NOT arithmetic addition or integration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tall Person" Problem

Imagine classifying people by **height**.

- A **crisp set** "Tall" might say: anyone $\ge 180$ cm is Tall, anyone $< 180$ cm is Not Tall.
- A **5 ft 11 in (180.3 cm)** person is abruptly classified the same as a **7 ft (213 cm)** person — both simply "Tall".
- A **5 ft 9 in (175 cm)** person is "Not Tall", despite being objectively much closer to "Tall" than to "short".

**Fuzzy logic fixes this absurdity.** It says:

| Person | Height (cm) | Crisp $\mu$ | Fuzzy $\mu_{\text{Tall}}$ |
|--------|:-----------:|:-----------:|:-------------------------:|
| Person A | 165 | 0 | 0.10 |
| Person B | 175 | 0 | 0.55 |
| Person C | 180 | 1 | 0.85 |
| Person D | 195 | 1 | 1.00 |

The fuzzy membership captures **gradual transition** — exactly how humans naturally reason using words like *"somewhat tall"*, *"very tall"*, *"slightly tall"*.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in optimization:** Many real-world engineering optimization problems (control systems, pattern recognition, economic forecasting) involve **vague, linguistic, or imprecise** parameters. Fuzzy logic provides a rigorous mathematical bridge between human-like linguistic reasoning and machine-computable numerics.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary (KTU High-Frequency)

- **Universe of Discourse ($X$):** The complete collection of all possible elements under consideration.
- **Membership Function $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$:** The curve/table that maps every $x \in X$ to a degree of membership in $[0, 1]$.
- **Support of $\tilde{A}$:** $\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{ x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0 \}$.
- **Core of $\tilde{A}$:** $\text{Core}(\tilde{A}) = \{ x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1 \}$.
- **Boundary:** $\text{Bound}(\tilde{A}) = \{ x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1 \}$.
- **Height $h(\tilde{A})$:** $\sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$. A fuzzy set is **normal** if $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$.
- **Crossover Point:** An $x_0$ where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_0) = 0.5$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operations on Fuzzy Sets

Let $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ be two fuzzy sets defined on the same universe $X$. The standard Zadeh operations are:

| Operation | Symbol | Membership Formula |
|-----------|:------:|:-------------------|
| Union | $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max \left( \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) \right)$ |
| Intersection | $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min \left( \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) \right)$ |
| Complement | $\tilde{A}^{c}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ |
| Algebraic Sum | $\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}$ | $\mu(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)\,\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$ |
| Algebraic Product | $\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B}$ | $\mu(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$ |
| Bounded Sum | $\tilde{A} \boxplus \tilde{B}$ | $\mu(x) = \min \left( 1,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) \right)$ |
| Bounded Difference | $\tilde{A} \boxminus \tilde{B}$ | $\mu(x) = \max \left( 0,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) - 1 \right)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **De Morgan's Laws** hold for Zadeh's operators:
> - $\left(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}\right)^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}$
> - $\left(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}\right)^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cup \tilde{B}^{c}$

## 2.2 $\alpha$-Cuts (Alpha Cuts) — The Crisp Extraction Bridge

> [!NOTE]
> $\alpha$-cuts are the most heavily tested concept on Fuzzy Logic in KTU examinations. They allow us to convert a fuzzy set into a family of nested crisp sets.

For a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ and a value $\alpha \in (0, 1]$:

**Strong (Strong) $\alpha$-cut:**

$$A_{\alpha} = \{ x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha \}$$

**Weak $\alpha$-cut:**

$$A_{\alpha}^{w} = \{ x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha \}$$

Key Properties:
1. If $\alpha_1 \leq \alpha_2$, then $A_{\alpha_1} \supseteq A_{\alpha_2}$ (nested).
2. $\bigcap_{\alpha} A_{\alpha} = \text{Core}(\tilde{A})$.
3. $\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha \in (0,1]} \alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$ (Resolution Identity).

## 2.3 Linguistic Variables & Hedges

A **Linguistic Variable** is a variable whose values are words or sentences in a natural or artificial language, rather than numbers.

Example: Variable = "Temperature"
- Term-set $T(\text{Temperature}) = \{ \text{cold},\ \text{cool},\ \text{warm},\ \text{hot} \}$
- Each term is a fuzzy set with its own membership function.

**Hedges** modify the meaning of fuzzy terms:
- *Very A*: $\mu_{\text{very } A}(x) = \left[\mu_{A}(x)\right]^{2}$
- *Extremely A*: $\mu_{\text{extremely } A}(x) = \left[\mu_{A}(x)\right]^{3}$
- *Slightly A*: $\mu_{\text{slightly } A}(x) = \sqrt{\mu_{A}(x)}$
- *More or less A*: $\mu_{\text{more or less } A}(x) = \sqrt[4]{\mu_{A}(x)}$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition |
|:-:|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Fuzzy set (discrete) | $\tilde{A} = \sum \mu_i / x_i$ |
| 2 | Union (max) | $\mu_{A \cup B} = \max(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ |
| 3 | Intersection (min) | $\mu_{A \cap B} = \min(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ |
| 4 | Complement | $\mu_{A^c} = 1 - \mu_A$ |
| 5 | Strong $\alpha$-cut | $A_{\alpha} = \{ x \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \alpha \}$ |
| 6 | Weak $\alpha$-cut | $A_{\alpha}^{w} = \{ x \mid \mu_A(x) > \alpha \}$ |
| 7 | Support | $\text{Supp}(A) = \{ x \mid \mu_A(x) > 0 \}$ |
| 8 | Core | $\text{Core}(A) = \{ x \mid \mu_A(x) = 1 \}$ |
| 9 | Height | $h(A) = \sup_x \mu_A(x)$ |
| 10 | Normality | $A$ is normal if $h(A) = 1$ |
| 11 | Cardinality | $\vert \tilde{A} \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_i$ |
| 12 | Concentration hedge | $\mu_{\text{Very } A} = \mu_A^{\,2}$ |
| 13 | Dilation hedge | $\mu_{\text{More or less } A} = \mu_A^{\,1/2}$ |
| 14 | Resolution identity | $\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha \in (0,1]} \alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$ |
| 15 | De Morgan (I) | $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$ |
| 16 | De Morgan (II) | $(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Fuzzy logic is the backbone of consumer appliances (washing machines, air-conditioners, autofocus cameras), automotive ABS systems, subway train control (Sendai, Japan — 1987), pattern recognition, medical diagnosis, and stock-market prediction algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Operations on Discrete Fuzzy Sets

**Problem (KTU Model):**
Let the universe of discourse be $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Two fuzzy sets are defined as:

$$\tilde{A} = \frac{0.1}{1} + \frac{0.4}{2} + \frac{0.8}{3} + \frac{1.0}{4} + \frac{0.6}{5}$$

$$\tilde{B} = \frac{0.3}{1} + \frac{0.7}{2} + \frac{0.9}{3} + \frac{0.5}{4} + \frac{0.2}{5}$$

**Required:** Compute (i) $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, (ii) $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, (iii) $\tilde{A}^{c}$, (iv) $A_{0.5}$, (v) Cardinality $\vert \tilde{A} \vert$.

---

### Part (i) Union $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$

Applying $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ element-wise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x=1:\quad & \max(0.1,\ 0.3) = 0.3 \\
x=2:\quad & \max(0.4,\ 0.7) = 0.7 \\
x=3:\quad & \max(0.8,\ 0.9) = 0.9 \\
x=4:\quad & \max(1.0,\ 0.5) = 1.0 \\
x=5:\quad & \max(0.6,\ 0.2) = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \frac{0.3}{1} + \frac{0.7}{2} + \frac{0.9}{3} + \frac{1.0}{4} + \frac{0.6}{5}$$

**Interpretation:** Each element receives the *stronger* membership grade from either set — analogous to OR in Boolean logic.

---

### Part (ii) Intersection $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$

Applying $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x=1:\quad & \min(0.1,\ 0.3) = 0.1 \\
x=2:\quad & \min(0.4,\ 0.7) = 0.4 \\
x=3:\quad & \min(0.8,\ 0.9) = 0.8 \\
x=4:\quad & \min(1.0,\ 0.5) = 0.5 \\
x=5:\quad & \min(0.6,\ 0.2) = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \frac{0.1}{1} + \frac{0.4}{2} + \frac{0.8}{3} + \frac{0.5}{4} + \frac{0.2}{5}$$

**Interpretation:** Each element receives the *weaker* of the two grades — analogous to AND.

---

### Part (iii) Complement $\tilde{A}^{c}$

Using $\mu_{A^c}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{A^c}(1) &= 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 \\
\mu_{A^c}(2) &= 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 \\
\mu_{A^c}(3) &= 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 \\
\mu_{A^c}(4) &= 1 - 1.0 = 0.0 \\
\mu_{A^c}(5) &= 1 - 0.6 = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\tilde{A}^{c} = \frac{0.9}{1} + \frac{0.6}{2} + \frac{0.2}{3} + \frac{0.0}{4} + \frac{0.4}{5}$$

**Verification of De Morgan:** Compute $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c}$ via element-wise $1 - \mu_{A \cup B}$:

$$(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c} = \frac{0.7}{1} + \frac{0.3}{2} + \frac{0.1}{3} + \frac{0.0}{4} + \frac{0.4}{5}$$

Now $\tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x=1:\quad & \min(0.9,\ 0.7) = 0.7 \\
x=2:\quad & \min(0.6,\ 0.3) = 0.3 \\
x=3:\quad & \min(0.2,\ 0.1) = 0.1 \\
x=4:\quad & \min(0.0,\ 0.5) = 0.0 \\
x=5:\quad & \min(0.4,\ 0.8) = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

Both results match → **De Morgan's Law I confirmed.** ✅

---

### Part (iv) Strong $\alpha$-Cut at $\alpha = 0.5$

$$A_{0.5} = \{ x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \geq 0.5 \}$$

Scanning $\mu_A$: $0.1 < 0.5$, $0.4 < 0.5$, $0.8 \geq 0.5$ ✔, $1.0 \geq 0.5$ ✔, $0.6 \geq 0.5$ ✔.

$$A_{0.5} = \{3,\ 4,\ 5\}$$

This is a **crisp subset** of $X$.

---

### Part (v) Cardinality $\vert \tilde{A} \vert$

$$\vert \tilde{A} \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \mu_A(x_i) = 0.1 + 0.4 + 0.8 + 1.0 + 0.6 = 2.9$$

This is also called the **sigma-count**.

## 3.2 Derivative Worked Example — $\alpha$-Cut Reconstruction

**Theorem (Resolution Identity):** Any fuzzy set can be reconstructed from its $\alpha$-cuts via:

$$\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha \in (0,1]} \alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$$

For $\tilde{A}$ from above, the $\alpha$-cuts over a suitable grid are:

| $\alpha$ | $A_{\alpha}$ | $\alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$ |
|:--------:|:------------:|:------------------------:|
| 1.0 | $\{4\}$ | $\{1.0/4\}$ |
| 0.8 | $\{3,\ 4\}$ | $\{0.8/3,\ 1.0/4\}$ |
| 0.6 | $\{3,\ 4,\ 5\}$ | $\{0.6/5\} + \ldots$ |
| 0.4 | $\{2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5\}$ | $\{0.4/2,\ldots\}$ |
| 0.1 | $\{1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5\}$ | $\{0.1/1,\ldots\}$ |

Taking the **union** (max across $\alpha$-levels) recovers the original fuzzy set element-by-element — confirming the resolution identity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Fuzzy Inference System (FIS)

A Fuzzy Logic System (FLS) is the optimization engine used in many control problems. Below is a complete functional flow showing how crisp inputs become crisp outputs through the fuzzy machinery.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    crispin["CRISP INPUTS x1 x2 x3"] --> fuzz["FUZZIFICATION MODULE"]
    fuzz --> mfdb["MEMBERSHIP FUNCTION DATABASE"]
    kb["RULE BASE KNOWLEDGE"] --> infeng["INFERENCE ENGINE"]
    mfdb --> infeng
    infeng --> defuzz["DEFUZZIFICATION MODULE"]
    defuzz --> crispout["CRISP OUTPUT y"]

    style fuzz fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style infeng fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0044aa
    style defuzz fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006622
    style kb fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#aa0000
    style mfdb fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#aa0000
```

**Explanation of each block:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **CRISP INPUTS** | Numerical sensor readings (e.g., temperature = 38.5 °C). |
| **FUZZIFICATION** | Converts each crisp input into one or more fuzzy membership grades using pre-defined MFs (triangular, trapezoidal, Gaussian). |
| **MEMBERSHIP FUNCTION DATABASE** | Repository of all MFs for all linguistic terms. |
| **RULE BASE** | IF–THEN rules supplied by human experts, e.g., `IF temp IS hot AND humidity IS high THEN fan_speed IS very_high`. |
| **INFERENCE ENGINE** | Matches fuzzified inputs against the rule base, fires rules, and combines the consequents (Mamdani / Sugeno methods). |
| **DEFUZZIFICATION** | Converts the aggregated fuzzy output back into a single crisp number (Centroid, Mean of Max, Bisector, Weighted Average). |
| **CRISP OUTPUT** | Final actionable value (e.g., fan speed = 78 %). |

## 4.2 Common Membership Function Shapes (Textual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TRIANGULAR MF trimf x a b c"] --> B["TRAPEZOIDAL MF trapmf x a b c d"]
    B --> C["GAUSSIAN MF gaussmf x sigma c"]
    C --> D["GENERALIZED BELL gbellmf x a b c"]
    D --> E["SIGMOIDAL MF sigmf x a c"]
    E --> F["S SHAPE AND Z SHAPE MF s z"]
```

**Canonical Equations:**

| MF | Formula |
|----|---------|
| Triangular | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0,\ \min\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a},\ \dfrac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$ |
| Trapezoidal | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0,\ \min\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a},\ 1,\ \dfrac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$ |
| Gaussian | $\mu(x) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ |
| Bell-shaped | $\mu(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left\vert \dfrac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}$ |
| Sigmoidal | $\mu(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-a(x-c)}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define a Fuzzy Set. How does it differ from a Crisp Set? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Fuzzy Set** $\tilde{A}$ on a universe $X$ is a set whose boundary is not sharp; it is defined by a membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}} : X \to [0, 1]$ assigning to each $x \in X$ a grade of membership.

**Key differences:**

| Feature | Crisp Set | Fuzzy Set |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Membership values | $\{0,\ 1\}$ | $[0,\ 1]$ (continuous) |
| Boundary | Sharp | Gradual |
| Logic basis | Boolean (two-valued) | Multi-valued (Zadeh) |
| Mathematical type | Set-theoretic | Set-theoretic + functional |

**Example:** Let $X = \{0, 10, 20, 30\}$ cm rainfall. Crisp set "Heavy Rain" = $\{20, 30\}$. Fuzzy set "Heavy Rain" = $\{0.0/0 + 0.2/10 + 0.8/20 + 1.0/30\}$.

*(Full 3 marks awarded for crisp definition + comparison table + valid example.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
**Define $\alpha$-cut and support of a fuzzy set. Find $A_{0.6}$ and $\text{Supp}(\tilde{A})$ for** $\tilde{A} = \dfrac{0.2}{1} + \dfrac{0.6}{2} + \dfrac{0.9}{3} + \dfrac{1.0}{4} + \dfrac{0.5}{5}$.

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions (1 mark each):**
- **$\alpha$-cut:** $A_{\alpha} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \alpha\}$.
- **Support:** $\text{Supp}(A) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\}$.

**Computation (1 mark):**

Elements with $\mu_A \geq 0.6$: $\{2,\ 3,\ 4\}$ → $\boxed{A_{0.6} = \{2,\ 3,\ 4\}}$.

Elements with $\mu_A > 0$: $\{1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5\}$ → $\boxed{\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5\}}$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define fuzzy set with suitable example. Discuss the various operations on fuzzy sets in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given two fuzzy sets on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$:
$$\tilde{A} = \frac{0.2}{1} + \frac{0.5}{2} + \frac{0.9}{3} + \frac{0.7}{4} + \frac{0.3}{5}, \quad \tilde{B} = \frac{0.4}{1} + \frac{0.6}{2} + \frac{0.8}{3} + \frac{0.5}{4} + \frac{0.1}{5}$$
Find (i) $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, (ii) $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, (iii) $\tilde{A}^{c}$, (iv) $A_{0.7}$, (v) Verify De Morgan's Law. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**
Full credit breakdown:
- Fuzzy set definition with Zadeh notation — **2 marks**
- Tabulated explanation of Union, Intersection, Complement — **2 marks**
- Brief mention of Algebraic Sum/Product and De Morgan's Law — **2 marks**
- Real-world example — **1 mark**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Union (max):**

$$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \frac{0.4}{1} + \frac{0.6}{2} + \frac{0.9}{3} + \frac{0.7}{4} + \frac{0.3}{5}$$

**(ii) Intersection (min):**

$$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \frac{0.2}{1} + \frac{0.5}{2} + \frac{0.8}{3} + \frac{0.5}{4} + \frac{0.1}{5}$$

**(iii) Complement of $\tilde{A}$:**

$$\tilde{A}^{c} = \frac{0.8}{1} + \frac{0.5}{2} + \frac{0.1}{3} + \frac{0.3}{4} + \frac{0.7}{5}$$

**(iv) Strong $0.7$-cut:** Elements where $\mu_A \geq 0.7$ → $\{3,\ 4\}$.
$$A_{0.7} = \{3,\ 4\}$$

**(v) De Morgan's Law verification:**

Compute $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c}$:
$$\frac{0.6}{1} + \frac{0.4}{2} + \frac{0.1}{3} + \frac{0.3}{4} + \frac{0.7}{5}$$

Compute $\tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}$ — first find $\tilde{B}^{c}$:

$$\tilde{B}^{c} = \frac{0.6}{1} + \frac{0.4}{2} + \frac{0.2}{3} + \frac{0.5}{4} + \frac{0.9}{5}$$

Now $\min$ element-wise:

$$\tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c} = \frac{0.6}{1} + \frac{0.4}{2} + \frac{0.1}{3} + \frac{0.3}{4} + \frac{0.7}{5}$$

✅ Matches $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c}$. Law verified.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of $\alpha$-cuts and Resolution Identity with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the fuzzy set $\tilde{A} = \dfrac{0.1}{1} + \dfrac{0.5}{2} + \dfrac{0.8}{3} + \dfrac{1.0}{4} + \dfrac{0.6}{5} + \dfrac{0.2}{6}$, determine (i) Support, (ii) Core, (iii) Crossover points, (iv) Height, (v) Boundary region, (vi) $A_{0.5}$ and $A^{w}_{0.5}$ (weak cut), (vii) Cardinality $\vert \tilde{A} \vert$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**
- Definition of strong and weak $\alpha$-cuts — **2 marks**
- Nested property $A_{\alpha_1} \supseteq A_{\alpha_2}$ for $\alpha_1 < \alpha_2$ — **2 marks**
- Resolution Identity statement and derivation sketch — **2 marks**
- Numerical illustrative example — **1 mark**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Property | Computation | Result |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| Support | $\{x \mid \mu_A > 0\}$ | $\{1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6\}$ |
| Core | $\{x \mid \mu_A = 1\}$ | $\{4\}$ |
| Crossover points | $\{x \mid \mu_A = 0.5\}$ | $\{2\}$ |
| Height | $\max \mu_A$ | $1.0$ (normal set) |
| Boundary | $\{x \mid 0 < \mu_A < 1\}$ | $\{1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 5,\ 6\}$ |
| Strong 0.5-cut | $\{x \mid \mu_A \geq 0.5\}$ | $\{2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5\}$ |
| Weak 0.5-cut | $\{x \mid \mu_A > 0.5\}$ | $\{3,\ 4,\ 5\}$ |
| Cardinality | $\sum \mu_i$ | $0.1+0.5+0.8+1.0+0.6+0.2 = 3.2$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Misuse of $\Sigma$ in Zadeh notation:** Many students write $\Sigma$ thinking it is arithmetic summation. It is **union of membership pairs** — no actual addition is performed.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the universe:** When defining $\tilde{A}$, you must state the underlying $X$ explicitly or risk losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **$\alpha$-cut direction confusion:** Strong cut uses $\geq$, weak cut uses $>$. Mixing them costs full marks for that sub-part.
> 4. **De Morgan verification:** Students often verify only one element instead of all — partial credit only.
> 5. **Core vs. Support confusion:** Core requires $\mu = 1$ exactly; Support requires $\mu > 0$ (strict).
> 6. **Missing the "Normal Set" remark:** If $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$, declare $\tilde{A}$ is **normal** explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Fuzzy set** = set with graded membership via $\mu: X \to [0, 1]$ (Zadeh, 1965).
- ✅ Crisp set values restricted to $\{0, 1\}$; Fuzzy set values continuous in $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **Union = max**, **Intersection = min**, **Complement = $1 - \mu$** (always Zadeh unless otherwise specified).
- ✅ **Strong $\alpha$-cut** uses $\geq$, **Weak $\alpha$-cut** uses $>$.
- ✅ **Core** = points where $\mu = 1$; **Support** = points where $\mu > 0$; **Boundary** = $0 < \mu < 1$.
- ✅ **Normal** fuzzy set has $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$.
- ✅ **Cardinality** = $\Sigma \mu_i$ (scalar sum).
- ✅ **De Morgan's Laws** hold for Zadeh operators — verify by element-wise comparison.
- ✅ **Resolution Identity:** $\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha} \alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$.
- ✅ **Hedges:** *Very* $\to \mu^2$; *More or less* $\to \sqrt{\mu}$; *Extremely* $\to \mu^3$; *Slightly* $\to \sqrt{\mu}$.
- ✅ **Linguistic variables** convert expert knowledge (words) into mathematical fuzzy sets.
- ✅ A **Fuzzy Inference System** = Fuzzifier + Rule Base + Inference Engine + Defuzzifier.
- ✅ $\alpha$-cuts are **nested**: smaller $\alpha$ → larger cut set.
- ✅ $\bigcap_{\alpha} A_{\alpha} = \text{Core}(\tilde{A})$ and $\bigcup_{\alpha} A_{\alpha}^{w} = \text{Supp}(\tilde{A})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuzzy Sets and Membership Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET755 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuzzy Sets and Membership Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classical (Crisp) Sets vs Fuzzy Sets

In classical set theory (developed by **Georg Cantor**), an element either **belongs** to a set or it **does not belong** to it. There is no intermediate state. This is mathematically represented by a **characteristic function** that maps every element to either **0** or **1**.

A **Fuzzy Set**, however, allows elements to belong to a set with **degrees of membership** ranging continuously from **0 to 1**. It was formally introduced by **Professor Lotfi A. Zadeh** in his seminal 1965 paper *"Fuzzy Sets"* in *Information and Control*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET755):**
> A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ in a universe of discourse $X$ is characterized by a **membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ that associates with each element $x \in X$ a real number in the closed interval $[0, 1]$, where the value indicates the **degree of belongingness** of $x$ to $\tilde{A}$.

### Formal Mathematical Definition

A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ in the universe of discourse $X$ is defined as a set of ordered pairs:

$$\tilde{A} = \left\{ (x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X \right\}$$

where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}: X \rightarrow [0, 1]$ is the **membership function (MF)** and $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ is the **membership grade (degree of membership)** of $x$ in $\tilde{A}$.

When $X$ is continuous, the set is written as:

$$\tilde{A} = \int_{X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) / x$$

When $X$ is discrete (finite):

$$\tilde{A} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_i) / x_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slash `/` in fuzzy notation is **not** a division operator. It is a **delimiter** separating the membership grade from the element.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Height of a Person" Problem

Imagine you are asked to classify people as **"tall"** or **"not tall"**.

| Person | Height | Classical Set (Tall?) | Fuzzy Set (Degree of Tallness) |
|--------|--------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Ravi | 1.55 m | NO (0) | 0.10 (barely tall) |
| Sita | 1.68 m | NO (0) | 0.55 (somewhat tall) |
| Arun | 1.80 m | YES (1) | 0.90 (very tall) |
| Vijay | 1.92 m | YES (1) | 1.00 (definitely tall) |

- **Classical reasoning** says a person of height $1.79$ m is **NOT tall**, while $1.80$ m **IS tall** — a discontinuous and unrealistic jump of just $1$ cm.
- **Fuzzy reasoning** assigns a smooth, gradual degree of tallness, capturing the inherent **vagueness** of real-world linguistic descriptions.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Fuzzy sets are the mathematical backbone of systems that mimic human reasoning — washing machines that detect "dirty clothes," air conditioners that sense "comfortable temperature," and automotive ABS systems that handle "slippery roads."

---

## 1.3 Why Fuzzy Sets Matter in Optimization

In real-world optimization (manufacturing, finance, supply chain, control systems), many parameters are **vague, imprecise, or linguistic** rather than crisp numerical values. Traditional LP/NLP methods struggle with such uncertainty. Fuzzy optimization bridges this gap by:

1. **Modeling linguistic variables** ("high profit," "low risk," "moderate demand") mathematically.
2. **Quantifying expert knowledge** that cannot be expressed in exact numbers.
3. **Producing robust solutions** that are insensitive to small data perturbations.
4. **Handling multi-criteria trade-offs** with human-like flexibility.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Membership Functions

A well-defined membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ must satisfy:

1. **Boundedness:** $0 \leq \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in X$.
2. **Normality:** $\exists\, x \in X$ such that $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$ (a fuzzy set must have at least one fully belonging element).
3. **Convexity (for convex fuzzy sets):** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \geq \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_1),\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_2))$ for all $\lambda \in [0,1]$.
4. **Support:** The set $\text{supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$.
5. **Core (Kernel):** The set $\text{core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$.
6. **Height:** $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$.
7. **Boundary:** The set $\text{bnd}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fuzzy set is called **normal** if $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$ and **sub-normal** otherwise. KTU questions frequently test the distinction between **convex** and **non-convex** fuzzy sets.

---

## 2.2 Types of Membership Functions (High-Yield for KTU)

### A. Triangular Membership Function (Trimf)
Defined by three parameters $\{a, b, c\}$ where $a < b < c$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a < x \leq b \\ \dfrac{c - x}{c - b}, & b < x < c \\ 0, & x \geq c \end{cases}$$

### B. Trapezoidal Membership Function (Trapmf)
Defined by four parameters $\{a, b, c, d\}$ where $a < b \leq c < d$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a < x \leq b \\ 1, & b < x \leq c \\ \dfrac{d - x}{d - c}, & c < x < d \\ 0, & x \geq d \end{cases}$$

### C. Gaussian Membership Function
Defined by parameters $\{c, \sigma\}$ where $c$ is the center and $\sigma > 0$ is the spread:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### D. Generalized Bell Membership Function
Defined by parameters $\{a, b, c\}$ where $a > 0$, $b > 0$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left|\dfrac{x - c}{a}\right|^{2b}}$$

### E. Sigmoid Membership Function
Defined by parameters $\{a, c\}$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp(-a(x - c))}$$

---

## 2.3 Operations on Fuzzy Sets (Kern-Items for ESE)

Let $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ be two fuzzy sets in $X$ with membership functions $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ and $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$.

| # | Operation | Symbol | Formula | Boundary Condition |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Union | $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$ | Values in $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | Intersection | $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$ | Values in $[0,1]$ |
| 3 | Complement | $\tilde{A}^c$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | Values in $[0,1]$ |
| 4 | Algebraic Sum | $\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}$ | $\mu(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$ | Bounded sum |
| 5 | Algebraic Product | $\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}$ | $\mu(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$ | Bounded product |
| 6 | Bounded Difference | $\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B}$ | $\mu(x) = \max(0,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) - \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$ | Non-negative |
| 7 | Bounded Sum | $\tilde{A} \boxplus \tilde{B}$ | $\mu(x) = \min(1,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$ | Capped at 1 |

---

## 2.4 α-Cuts (Alpha-Cuts) and Strict α-Cuts

The **α-cut** (or **λ-cut**) of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is the **crisp set** of all elements whose membership grade is **greater than or equal to** a given threshold $\alpha \in [0,1]$.

$$\text{Strong } \alpha\text{-cut:}\ \ A_{\alpha} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$$

$$\text{Weak } \alpha\text{-cut:}\ \ A_{\alpha}^{+} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special cases:**
> - $A_0 = X$ (the entire universe) and $A_0^+ = \text{supp}(\tilde{A})$.
> - $A_1 = \text{core}(\tilde{A})$.
> - $\alpha_1 \leq \alpha_2 \Rightarrow A_{\alpha_1} \supseteq A_{\alpha_2}$ (monotonicity).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

| Concept | Symbol / Notation | Key Expression | Important Condition |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Membership function | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}: X \rightarrow [0,1]$ | Range bounded |
| Core of fuzzy set | $\text{core}(\tilde{A})$ | $\{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$ | Non-empty for normal sets |
| Support of fuzzy set | $\text{supp}(\tilde{A})$ | $\{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ | Excludes zeros |
| Height of fuzzy set | $h(\tilde{A})$ | $\sup_x \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | Equals $1$ for normal sets |
| Strong α-cut | $A_{\alpha}$ | $\{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$ | Closed crisp set |
| Weak α-cut | $A_{\alpha}^{+}$ | $\{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha\}$ | Open crisp set |
| Card(Scalar cardinality) | $\mid \tilde{A} \mid$ | $\sum_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | Sum of grades |
| Triangular MF area | $A_{\triangle}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}(c - a) \cdot h$ | Useful in defuzzification |

---

| **Control Systems** | Error, change-in-error fuzzification in FLC |

| **Finance** | Modeling "high-risk" portfolios |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Construction of a Fuzzy Set

**Problem:** Let $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60\}$ represent the daily demand (in units) of a product. A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ = "moderate demand" is defined with the following membership grades:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(10) = 0.1,\ \ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(20) = 0.4,\ \ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(30) = 0.9$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(40) = 1.0,\ \ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(50) = 0.6,\ \ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(60) = 0.2$$

**Step 1: Express $\tilde{A}$ in Zadeh's notation.**

$$\tilde{A} = \dfrac{0.1}{10} + \dfrac{0.4}{20} + \dfrac{0.9}{30} + \dfrac{1.0}{40} + \dfrac{0.6}{50} + \dfrac{0.2}{60}$$

**Step 2: Determine the support of $\tilde{A}$.**

$$\text{supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\} = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60\}$$

because all membership grades are strictly positive (none equal $0$).

**Step 3: Determine the core of $\tilde{A}$.**

$$\text{core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\} = \{40\}$$

**Step 4: Determine the height of $\tilde{A}$.**

$$h(\tilde{A}) = \max_x \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \max(0.1,\ 0.4,\ 0.9,\ 1.0,\ 0.6,\ 0.2) = 1.0$$

Since $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$, the fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is **normal**.

**Step 5: Determine the boundary of $\tilde{A}$.**

$$\text{bnd}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1\} = \{10,\ 20,\ 30,\ 50,\ 60\}$$

**Step 6: Find the $0.5$-cut (strong α-cut).**

$$A_{0.5} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq 0.5\} = \{30,\ 40,\ 50\}$$

**Step 7: Find the $0.5^{+}$-cut (weak α-cut).**

$$A_{0.5}^{+} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0.5\} = \{30,\ 40,\ 50\}$$

(In this example both coincide because no element has a grade *exactly* equal to $0.5$.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fuzzy Set Operations

**Problem:** Given two fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ in $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$:

$$\tilde{A} = \dfrac{0.2}{x_1} + \dfrac{0.7}{x_2} + \dfrac{0.9}{x_3}$$
$$\tilde{B} = \dfrac{0.6}{x_1} + \dfrac{0.4}{x_2} + \dfrac{0.5}{x_3}$$

Compute $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, $\tilde{A}^c$, $\tilde{B}^c$, and the algebraic product $\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}$.

### Step 1: Union $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$

Using the max operator $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_1) = \max(0.2,\ 0.6) = 0.6$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_2) = \max(0.7,\ 0.4) = 0.7$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_3) = \max(0.9,\ 0.5) = 0.9$$

$$\therefore\ \tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \dfrac{0.6}{x_1} + \dfrac{0.7}{x_2} + \dfrac{0.9}{x_3}$$

### Step 2: Intersection $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$

Using the min operator $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_1) = \min(0.2,\ 0.6) = 0.2$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_2) = \min(0.7,\ 0.4) = 0.4$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_3) = \min(0.9,\ 0.5) = 0.5$$

$$\therefore\ \tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \dfrac{0.2}{x_1} + \dfrac{0.4}{x_2} + \dfrac{0.5}{x_3}$$

### Step 3: Complements $\tilde{A}^c$ and $\tilde{B}^c$

Using $\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$:

$$\tilde{A}^c = \dfrac{0.8}{x_1} + \dfrac{0.3}{x_2} + \dfrac{0.1}{x_3}$$
$$\tilde{B}^c = \dfrac{0.4}{x_1} + \dfrac{0.6}{x_2} + \dfrac{0.5}{x_3}$$

### Step 4: Algebraic Product $\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}$

Using $\mu(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(x_1) = (0.2)(0.6) = 0.12$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(x_2) = (0.7)(0.4) = 0.28$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(x_3) = (0.9)(0.5) = 0.45$$

$$\therefore\ \tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B} = \dfrac{0.12}{x_1} + \dfrac{0.28}{x_2} + \dfrac{0.45}{x_3}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Triangular Membership Function Numerical

**Problem:** A fuzzy set "**Optimal Temperature**" has a triangular MF with parameters $a = 15^\circ\text{C}$, $b = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $c = 35^\circ\text{C}$. Compute $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(20)$ and $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(30)$.

**Solution framework:** For $a < x \leq b$, the formula is $\mu(x) = \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}$.

**Step 1:** Substitute $x = 20$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(20) = \dfrac{20 - 15}{25 - 15} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.50$$

**Step 2:** For $b < x < c$, use $\mu(x) = \dfrac{c - x}{c - b}$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(30) = \dfrac{35 - 30}{35 - 25} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.50$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Verification — De Morgan's Laws for Fuzzy Sets

De Morgan's laws hold under the **min-max** system. For fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$:

$$\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \overline{\tilde{A}} \cap \overline{\tilde{B}}$$
$$\overline{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}} = \overline{\tilde{A}} \cup \overline{\tilde{B}}$$

**Proof (LHS of Law 1):**

$$\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = 1 - \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$

$$\text{Using identity: } 1 - \max(a,b) = \min(1 - a,\ 1 - b)$$

$$\Rightarrow 1 - \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)) = \min(1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ 1 - \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$

$$= \min(\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}}}(x),\ \mu_{\overline{\tilde{B}}}(x)) = \mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}} \cap \overline{\tilde{B}}}(x)$$

Thus $\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \overline{\tilde{A}} \cap \overline{\tilde{B}}$. $\blacksquare$
