# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Fuzzy Set. Distinguish between a classical (crisp) set and a fuzzy set with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **fuzzy set** $\tilde{A}$ in a universe of discourse $X$ is a collection of objects $x \in X$ characterized by a **membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ that assigns to each element a grade of membership in the closed interval $[0, 1]$.

$$\tilde{A} = \{ (x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X \}$$

**Key Differences:**

| Property | Classical (Crisp) Set | Fuzzy Set |
|----------|------------------------|-----------|
| Membership function range | $\{0, 1\}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Belongingness | Binary (yes/no) | Gradual (degree-based) |
| Boundary | Sharp/crisp | Smooth |
| Handling of vagueness | Poor | Excellent |

**Example:** "People aged above 18 are adults" is a classical statement. In contrast, "people around 18" describes a fuzzy set with peak membership at $x = 18$ and decreasing values on either side.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the concept of α-cut of a fuzzy set with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **α-cut** (also called **λ-cut** or **level set**) of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is the **crisp set** containing all elements of $X$ whose membership grades are greater than or equal to a specified threshold $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.

**Strong α-cut:**
$$A_{\alpha} = \{ x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha \}$$

**Weak α-cut:**
$$A_{\alpha}^{+} = \{ x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha \}$$

**Example:** For $\tilde{A} = \dfrac{0.2}{10} + \dfrac{0.5}{20} + \dfrac{0.8}{30} + \dfrac{1.0}{40} + \dfrac{0.3}{50}$, the $0.5$-cut is $A_{0.5} = \{20, 30, 40\}$ and the $0.8$-cut is $A_{0.8} = \{30, 40\}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **strong α-cut** (uses $\geq$) with the **weak α-cut** (uses $>$). Marks are lost when the threshold value itself coincides with an existing membership grade.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Define the **triangular** and **trapezoidal** membership functions. Sketch their graphs and explain the significance of each parameter. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ are given by:
$$\tilde{A} = \dfrac{0.1}{1} + \dfrac{0.4}{2} + \dfrac{0.8}{3} + \dfrac{1.0}{4} + \dfrac{0.6}{5}$$
$$\tilde{B} = \dfrac{0.3}{1} + \dfrac{0.7}{2} + \dfrac{0.5}{3} + \dfrac{0.2}{4} + \dfrac{0.9}{5}$$
Find $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, $\tilde{A}^c$, and $\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Triangular MF (Trimf):** Characterized by a triple $(a, b, c)$ where $a \leq b \leq c$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a < x \leq b \\ \dfrac{c - x}{c - b}, & b < x < c \\ 0, & x \geq c \end{cases}$$

**Trapezoidal MF (Trapmf):** Characterized by a quadruple $(a, b, c, d)$ where $a < b \leq c < d$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a < x \leq b \\ 1, & b < x \leq c \\ \dfrac{d - x}{d - c}, & c < x < d \\ 0, & x \geq d \end{cases}$$

**Parameter significance:**
- $a$ = left foot (membership begins rising from 0)
- $b$ = left shoulder (membership reaches 1)
- $c$ = right shoulder (membership starts falling from 1)
- $d$ = right foot (membership returns to 0)

For **trimf**, $b = c$ (a single peak point). For **trapmf**, $b < c$ (a flat plateau of full membership).

**Graphical sketch (ASCII):**

```
Triangular MF (a=2, b=5, c=8):          Trapezoidal MF (a=1, b=3, c=6, d=9):

   1 |       /\                              1 |     __________
     |      /  \                               |    /          \
     |     /    \                              |   /            \
   0 |____/______\____                         0 |__/____________\____
        2   5    8                                1  3   6       9
```

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Union** $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ (max operator):

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$

| $x$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$ | $\max$ |
|-----|----------------------|----------------------|--------|
| 1 | 0.1 | 0.3 | 0.3 |
| 2 | 0.4 | 0.7 | 0.7 |
| 3 | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.8 |
| 4 | 1.0 | 0.2 | 1.0 |
| 5 | 0.6 | 0.9 | 0.9 |

$$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \dfrac{0.3}{1} + \dfrac{0.7}{2} + \dfrac{0.8}{3} + \dfrac{1.0}{4} + \dfrac{0.9}{5}$$

**(ii) Intersection** $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ (min operator):

| $x$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$ | $\min$ |
|-----|----------------------|----------------------|--------|
| 1 | 0.1 | 0.3 | 0.1 |
| 2 | 0.4 | 0.7 | 0.4 |
| 3 | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.5 |
| 4 | 1.0 | 0.2 | 0.2 |
| 5 | 0.6 | 0.9 | 0.6 |

$$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \dfrac{0.1}{1} + \dfrac{0.4}{2} + \dfrac{0.5}{3} + \dfrac{0.2}{4} + \dfrac{0.6}{5}$$

**(iii) Complement** $\tilde{A}^c$ using $\mu(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$:

$$\tilde{A}^c = \dfrac{0.9}{1} + \dfrac{0.6}{2} + \dfrac{0.2}{3} + \dfrac{0.0}{4} + \dfrac{0.4}{5}$$

**(iv) Algebraic Product** $\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(1) = 0.1 \times 0.3 = 0.03$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(2) = 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.28$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(3) = 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.40$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(4) = 1.0 \times 0.2 = 0.20$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(5) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$$

$$\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B} = \dfrac{0.03}{1} + \dfrac{0.28}{2} + \dfrac{0.40}{3} + \dfrac{0.20}{4} + \dfrac{0.54}{5}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Define **support, core, height, and boundary** of a fuzzy set. With reference to the fuzzy set
$$\tilde{A} = \dfrac{0.0}{1} + \dfrac{0.2}{2} + \dfrac{0.6}{3} + \dfrac{1.0}{4} + \dfrac{0.7}{5} + \dfrac{0.3}{6} + \dfrac{0.0}{7}$$
compute the support, core, height, and boundary. Also determine the $0.5$-cut. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Gaussian** and **Generalized Bell** membership functions with mathematical expressions and sketches. Compare their suitability for different applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definitions:**
- **Support** $\text{supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ — elements with strictly positive membership.
- **Core** $\text{core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$ — elements with full membership.
- **Height** $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_x \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ — supremum of all membership grades.
- **Boundary** $\text{bnd}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid 0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1\}$ — elements with partial membership.

**Step-by-step computation:**

**Step 1 — Support:**
$$\text{supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\} = \{2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6\}$$
(The elements $1$ and $7$ have membership exactly $0$, hence excluded.)

**Step 2 — Core:**
$$\text{core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\} = \{4\}$$

**Step 3 — Height:**
$$h(\tilde{A}) = \max(0.0,\ 0.2,\ 0.6,\ 1.0,\ 0.7,\ 0.3,\ 0.0) = 1.0$$
Hence $\tilde{A}$ is a **normal** fuzzy set.

**Step 4 — Boundary:**
$$\text{bnd}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1\} = \{2,\ 3,\ 5,\ 6\}$$

**Step 5 — $0.5$-cut (strong α-cut):**
$$A_{0.5} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq 0.5\} = \{3,\ 4,\ 5\}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Gaussian Membership Function:** Defined by center $c$ and standard deviation $\sigma > 0$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

- $c$ = center (peak of the curve, $\mu(c) = 1$).
- $\sigma$ = spread/width controlling how rapidly the membership decays.
- The function is **smooth, symmetric, infinitely differentiable**, and never reaches exactly $0$.

**Generalized Bell Membership Function (Gbellmf):** Defined by parameters $a > 0$, $b > 0$, $c \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left|\dfrac{x - c}{a}\right|^{2b}}$$

- $c$ = center (peak at $\mu(c) = 1$).
- $a$ = half-width controlling the spread.
- $b$ = slope/steepness control (larger $b$ → sharper transitions).

**Graphical sketches (ASCII):**

```
Gaussian MF (c=5, sigma=1.5):      Generalized Bell MF (a=2, b=2, c=5):
   1 |      ____                       1 |    /\
     |    /      \                       |   /  \
     |   /        \                      |  /    \
   0 |__/__________\___                 0 |_/______\_______
       2  3 4 5 6 7                         2 3 4 5 6 7
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Gaussian MF | Generalized Bell MF |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| Smoothness | $C^\infty$ smooth | $C^0$ continuous |
| Parameters | 2 ($c, \sigma$) | 3 ($a, b, c$) |
| Tunability | Limited (width only) | High (width + slope) |
| Computational cost | Low | Moderate (uses absolute value & power) |
| Typical use | Noise modeling, smooth control | Fuzzy classifiers, adaptive systems |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently forget to explicitly identify the **parameters** and their **physical significance** when explaining MFs. Marks are awarded for: (1) the correct mathematical expression, (2) the labelled diagram with parameter callouts, and (3) the parameter interpretation in plain English.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEMET755 Module 4**

- [ ] A **fuzzy set** is fully defined by its **membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0,1]$; classical sets are a special case with $\mu \in \{0, 1\}$.
- [ ] Zadeh's 1965 paper introduced fuzzy sets to handle **vagueness** rather than randomness.
- [ ] **Support** = strictly positive grades; **Core** = grade equal to $1$; **Boundary** = strictly between $0$ and $1$.
- [ ] A fuzzy set with $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$ is **normal**; otherwise it is **sub-normal**.
- [ ] **Union** uses $\max$; **Intersection** uses $\min$; **Complement** uses $1 - \mu$.
- [ ] **De Morgan's laws** hold under the **min-max** system but fail under algebraic sum/product.
- [ ] **α-cuts** are crisp sets; $\alpha_1 \leq \alpha_2 \Rightarrow A_{\alpha_1} \supseteq A_{\alpha_2}$.
- [ ] **Triangular MF** needs 3 parameters; **Trapezoidal MF** needs 4 parameters.
- [ ] **Gaussian MF** is best for smooth, symmetric membership; **Generalized Bell MF** offers tunable slope.
- [ ] In **fuzzy optimization**, MFs quantify linguistic goals like "approximately 50" or "much greater than 100."
- [ ] Remember to write fuzzy sets using **Zadeh's notation** $\sum \mu_i / x_i$ — the slash is **not** division.
- [ ] Always **verify normality, convexity, and boundedness** when characterizing a fuzzy set in KTU answers.
- [ ] Be ready to compute **scalar cardinality** $\sum \mu_i$ and the **centroid** $\dfrac{\sum \mu_i x_i}{\sum \mu_i}$ for defuzzification-based questions.

---

*End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Fuzzy Sets and Membership Functions.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations on Fuzzy Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET755 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Operations on Fuzzy Sets # MODULE 4 — OPERATIONS ON FUZZY SETS ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **FUZZY SET — OPERATIONAL DEFINITION** > A **fuzzy set** $\tilde{A}$ defined on a universal discourse $X$ is characterized by a **membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0, 1]$, which assigns to every element $x \in X$ a **grade of membership** indicating the *degree* to which $x$ belongs to $\tilde{A}$. The fuzzy set is formally written as: > $$\tilde{A} = \{\, (x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X \,\}$$ Unlike crisp (classical) sets where an element either belongs ($1$) or does not belong ($0$), fuzzy sets permit a *continuous spectrum* of belongingness. Operations on fuzzy sets therefore operate element-wise on these membership values, producing a new fuzzy set $\tilde{C}$ via an operation $\star$ such that: $$\mu_{\tilde{C}}(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \ \star \ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x), \quad \forall x \in X$$ The three **primitive (canonical) operations** are: - **Complement** $\neg \tilde{A}$ (NOT operation) - **Intersection** $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ (AND operation) - **Union** $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ (OR operation) These operations are generalisations of crisp set operations but are **NOT uniquely defined** — there are infinitely many valid fuzzy operators, classified into families called **T-norms** (intersection), **T-conorms / S-norms** (union), and **negators / fuzzy complements**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **REAL-WORLD ANALOGY: "How tall is 'tall'?"** Imagine a university admission committee evaluating a candidate as *"tall enough"*. A classical (crisp) set would say: if $height \geq 180\text{ cm}$, then **tall = YES** (membership 1); otherwise **tall = NO** (membership 0). But reality is gradient — a person of $179\text{ cm}$ is *almost* tall, $175\text{ cm}$ is *partially* tall, and $150\text{ cm}$ is *barely* tall. **Fuzzy set operations work on these gradient grades.** When the committee asks *"Is the candidate both tall AND smart?"*, fuzzy AND (intersection) combines the two grades — typically by taking the *smaller* one (the more limiting factor). When they ask *"Is the candidate tall OR charismatic?"*, fuzzy OR (union) combines them by taking the *larger* one (the strongest evidence). When they ask *"Is the candidate NOT tall?"*, fuzzy NOT (complement) simply subtracts the grade from 1. This element-wise combination is what we call an **operation on fuzzy sets**. --- ## 1.3 The Three Primitive Fuzzy Operators (Standard / Canonical) Let $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}$ be two fuzzy sets on $X$ with $a = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$, $b = \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$, where $a, b \in [0, 1]$. | Operator | Symbol | Standard Formula | |---|---|---| | **Complement** | $\bar{A}$ or $\tilde{A}^{c}$ | $\mu_{\bar{A}}(x) = 1 - a$ | | **Intersection** (Standard / Min) | $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(a,\ b)$ | | **Union** (Standard / Max) | $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(a,\ b)$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU KEY INSIGHT** > The *standard* fuzzy operations (Zadeh, 1965) generalise the crisp Boolean operations by replacing AND with **min** and OR with **max**. They are the **most commonly expected answer** in KTU examinations unless the question explicitly specifies an algebraic, bounded, or drastic variant. --- ## 1.4 Why Standard Operations Are Not Unique Crisp Boolean algebra admits a *unique* AND, OR, NOT. In fuzzy logic, this uniqueness collapses because: 1. We must satisfy infinitely many boundary cases (memberships ranging continuously over $[0,1]$). 2. The axioms chosen (e.g., De Morgan's law, distributivity) may or may not be preserved. 3. Different engineering applications demand different sensitivity to membership values. Hence, the literature defines **families** of operators with **parametrised** or **specialised** behaviour — these are discussed in detail in **SECTION 2**. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — FUZZY OPERATOR FAMILIES ## 2.1 Axiomatic Foundation — T-Norms and T-Conorms ### 2.1.1 T-Norm (Triangular Norm) — Intersection Family > [!IMPORTANT] > **T-NORM DEFINITION (KTU EXPECTED FORMULATION)** > A **T-norm** $\top : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ is a binary operation satisfying for all $a, b, c \in [0,1]$: > 1. **Boundary:** $\top(a, 1) = a$ and $\top(a, 0) = 0$ > 2. **Commutativity:** $\top(a, b) = \top(b, a)$ > 3. **Monotonicity:** if $a \le c$ and $b \le d$, then $\top(a, b) \le \top(c, d)$ > 4. **Associativity:** $\top(a, \top(b, c)) = \top(\top(a, b), c)$ It is used to model **fuzzy AND (intersection)**. ### 2.1.2 T-Conorm (S-Norm) — Union Family A **T-conorm** $\bot : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ satisfies: 1. **Boundary:** $\bot(a, 0) = a$ and $\bot(a, 1) = 1$ 2. **Commutativity, Monotonicity, Associativity** as above. It is used to model **fuzzy OR (union)**. ### 2.1.3 Negator (Fuzzy Complement) A function $N : [0,1] \to [0,1]$ is a **negator** if: - $N(0) = 1$, $N(1) = 0$ (boundary) - $N$ is monotonically non-increasing The **standard negator** is $N(a) = 1 - a$. --- ## 2.2 The "Big Four" Intersection Families (KTU High-Yield) For any two membership values $a, b \in [0,1]$, the following intersection operators are exhaustively tested in KTU examinations: ### (i) Standard / Minimum Intersection $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(a, b)$$ ### (ii) Algebraic Product $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = a \cdot b$$ ### (iii) Bounded Product (Łukasiewicz T-norm) $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(0,\ a + b - 1)$$ ### (iv) Drastic Product $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } b = 1 \\ b & \text{if } a = 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ --- ## 2.3 The "Big Four" Union Families ### (i) Standard / Maximum Union $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(a, b)$$ ### (ii) Algebraic Sum $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = a + b - a \cdot b$$ ### (iii) Bounded Sum (Łukasiewicz T-conorm) $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(1,\ a + b)$$ ### (iv) Drastic Sum $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } b = 0 \\ b & \text{if } a = 0 \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ > [!NOTE] > **MEMORY AID FOR KTU STUDENTS** > Notice that **drastic operators** depend on whether the *other* operand is at the extreme ($0$ or $1$). If neither is extreme, drastic intersection gives $0$ and drastic union gives $1$. This is their most distinctive — and most testable — feature. --- ## 2.4 Additional Negator Families | Negator | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---| | **Standard** | $N(a) = 1 - a$ | Involution: $N(N(a)) = a$ | | **Sugeno / Yager** | $N_w(a) = \dfrac{1 - a}{1 + w \cdot a}$, $w > -1$ | Parametric; $w = 0$ reduces to standard | | **Yager** | $N_w(a) = (1 - a^w)^{1/w}$, $w > 0$ | Reduces to standard as $w \to \infty$ | --- ## 2.5 Properties of Fuzzy Set Operations (Examination Gold Mine) Let $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}, \tilde{C} \subseteq X$. Using the *standard* (min/max) operators: | Property | Crisp | Fuzzy (Standard) | Holds? | |---|---|---|---| | **Commutativity** | $A \cap B = B \cap A$ | $\min(a,b) = \min(b,a)$ | ✅ YES | | **Associativity** | $(A \cap B) \cap C = A \cap (B \cap C)$ | Holds via $\min$ associativity | ✅ YES | | **Idempotence** | $A \cap A = A$ | $\min(a, a) = a$ | ✅ YES | | **Distributivity** | $A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)$ | $\min(a, \max(b,c)) = \max(\min(a,b), \min(a,c))$ | ✅ YES | | **De Morgan's Laws** | $\overline{A \cap B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B}$ | $1 - \min(a,b) = \max(1-a, 1-b)$ | ✅ YES | | **Identity** | $A \cap X = A$, $A \cup \emptyset = A$ | $\min(a,1) = a$, $\max(a,0) = a$ | ✅ YES | | **Domination** | $A \cup X = X$ | $\max(a, 1) = 1$ | ✅ YES | | **Involution** | $\overline{\bar{A}} = A$ | $1 - (1 - a) = a$ | ✅ YES (for Standard negator) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL — READ CAREFULLY** > The **Law of Excluded Middle** ($\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^c = X$) and the **Law of Non-Contradiction** ($\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{A}^c = \emptyset$) **FAIL** for standard fuzzy operations (except at extremes). Specifically: > $$\max(a,\ 1-a) \neq 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \min(a,\ 1-a) \neq 0 \quad \text{in general.}$$ > This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark conceptual question**. The failure stems from the fact that membership is *graded*, so an element can simultaneously have a *partial* truth value for both $A$ and $\neg A$. --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Table) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORISE FOR EXAM DAY** | Operation | Crisp (Boolean) | Fuzzy Standard | Algebraic | Bounded | Drastic | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **NOT** ($\bar{A}$) | $1 - a$ | $1 - a$ | — | — | — | | **AND** ($\cap$) | $\min(a,b)$ | $\min(a,b)$ | $a \cdot b$ | $\max(0, a+b-1)$ | $a$ if $b=1$, $b$ if $a=1$, else $0$ | | **OR** ($\cup$) | $\max(a,b)$ | $\max(a,b)$ | $a + b - ab$ | $\min(1, a+b)$ | $a$ if $b=0$, $b$ if $a=0$, else $1$ | | **Implication** | $\max(1-a, b)$ | — | — | $\min(1, 1-a+b)$ | — | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **APPLICATIONS — WHY THIS MATTERS IN ENGINEERING** - **Control Systems:** Fuzzy logic controllers in washing machines, ACs, and ABS braking use fuzzy AND/OR to combine sensor inputs (e.g., temperature AND humidity → fan speed). - **Decision Support:** Medical diagnosis expert systems combine symptom fuzzy sets via min (intersection) to obtain disease likelihood. - **Pattern Recognition:** Image edge detection uses fuzzy morphological operations (min/max dilations). - **Industrial Optimisation:** Production scheduling where constraints have soft (fuzzy) bounds — operations compute the *degree of constraint satisfaction*. - **AI / NLP:** Fuzzy matching in search engines combines keyword relevance scores using T-norms. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Standard Operations on Discrete Fuzzy Sets > [!IMPORTANT] > **WORKED PROBLEM (MODEL KTU 14-MARK PATTERN)** **Problem:** Given two fuzzy sets on $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$: $$\tilde{A} = \left\{\frac{0.2}{x_1},\ \frac{0.7}{x_2},\ \frac{1.0}{x_3},\ \frac{0.5}{x_4}\right\}, \quad \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.6}{x_1},\ \frac{0.4}{x_2},\ \frac{0.9}{x_3},\ \frac{0.3}{x_4}\right\}$$ Compute (i) $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ (standard), (ii) $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ (standard), (iii) $\tilde{A}^c$, (iv) verify De Morgan's Law for this universe. ### Step (i): Standard Intersection $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ By definition, $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$. Element-wise evaluation: $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_1) = \min(0.2,\ 0.6) = 0.2$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_2) = \min(0.7,\ 0.4) = 0.4$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_3) = \min(1.0,\ 0.9) = 0.9$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_4) = \min(0.5,\ 0.3) = 0.3$$ Result: $$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.2}{x_1},\ \frac{0.4}{x_2},\ \frac{0.9}{x_3},\ \frac{0.3}{x_4}\right\}$$ ### Step (ii): Standard Union $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ By definition, $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$. $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_1) = \max(0.2,\ 0.6) = 0.6$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_2) = \max(0.7,\ 0.4) = 0.7$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_3) = \max(1.0,\ 0.9) = 1.0$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_4) = \max(0.5,\ 0.3) = 0.5$$ Result: $$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.6}{x_1},\ \frac{0.7}{x_2},\ \frac{1.0}{x_3},\ \frac{0.5}{x_4}\right\}$$ ### Step (iii): Complement $\tilde{A}^c$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$ $$\tilde{A}^c = \left\{\frac{0.8}{x_1},\ \frac{0.3}{x_2},\ \frac{0.0}{x_3},\ \frac{0.5}{x_4}\right\}$$ ### Step (iv): Verify De Morgan's Law De Morgan's Law states: $$\overline{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}} = \tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c$$ **Compute LHS** — complement of intersection from Step (i): $$\overline{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}} = \left\{\frac{0.8}{x_1},\ \frac{0.6}{x_2},\ \frac{0.1}{x_3},\ \frac{0.7}{x_4}\right\}$$ **Compute RHS** — first find $\tilde{B}^c$: $$\tilde{B}^c = \left\{\frac{0.4}{x_1},\ \frac{0.6}{x_2},\ \frac{0.1}{x_3},\ \frac{0.7}{x_4}\right\}$$ Then compute $\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c$: $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c}(x_1) = \max(0.8,\ 0.4) = 0.8$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c}(x_2) = \max(0.3,\ 0.6) = 0.6$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c}(x_3) = \max(0.0,\ 0.1) = 0.1$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c}(x_4) = \max(0.5,\ 0.7) = 0.7$$ Result: $$\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c = \left\{\frac{0.8}{x_1},\ \frac{0.6}{x_2},\ \frac{0.1}{x_3},\ \frac{0.7}{x_4}\right\}$$ **LHS = RHS**, confirming De Morgan's Law holds for the standard (min/max) fuzzy operators. ✅ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Algebraic Operations on Fuzzy Sets **Same $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}$ as above.** Compute (i) $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ (algebraic product), (ii) $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ (algebraic sum). ### Step (i): Algebraic Product Intersection $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = a \cdot b$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_1) = 0.2 \times 0.6 = 0.12$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_2) = 0.7 \times 0.4 = 0.28$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_3) = 1.0 \times 0.9 = 0.90$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_4) = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15$$ Result: $$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} \ (\text{algebraic}) = \left\{\frac{0.12}{x_1},\ \frac{0.28}{x_2},\ \frac{0.90}{x_3},\ \frac{0.15}{x_4}\right\}$$ ### Step (ii): Algebraic Sum Union $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = a + b - a \cdot b$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_1) = 0.2 + 0.6 - (0.2)(0.6) = 0.80 - 0.12 = 0.68$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_2) = 0.7 + 0.4 - (0.7)(0.4) = 1.10 - 0.28 = 0.82$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_3) = 1.0 + 0.9 - (1.0)(0.9) = 1.90 - 0.90 = 1.00$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_4) = 0.5 + 0.3 - (0.5)(0.3) = 0.80 - 0.15 = 0.65$$ Result: $$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} \ (\text{algebraic}) = \left\{\frac{0.68}{x_1},\ \frac{0.82}{x_2},\ \frac{1.00}{x_3},\ \frac{0.65}{x_4}\right\}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bounded & Drastic Operations **Same $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}$ as above.** Compute Bounded Intersection and Drastic Union for element $x_2$. ### Bounded Intersection at $x_2$ (a = 0.7, b = 0.4) $$\mu_{\text{bounded}}(x_2) = \max(0,\ a + b - 1) = \max(0,\ 0.7 + 0.4 - 1) = \max(0,\ 0.1) = 0.1$$ ### Drastic Union at $x_2$ (a = 0.7, b = 0.4) Since neither $a = 0$ nor $b = 0$, the drastic sum evaluates to: $$\mu_{\text{drastic}}(x_2) = 1$$ > [!NOTE] > **OBSERVATION — ORDERING OF T-NORMS** > For any $a, b \in [0,1]$ excluding boundary, the following ordering holds (KTU standard result): > $$T_{\text{drastic}}(a,b) \le T_{\text{bounded}}(a,b) \le T_{\text{algebraic}}(a,b) \le T_{\min}(a,b)$$ > Similarly, T-conorms satisfy the reverse ordering: > $$S_{\max}(a,b) \le S_{\text{algebraic}}(a,b) \le S_{\text{bounded}}(a,b) \le S_{\text{drastic}}(a,b)$$ --- ## 3.4 Failure of Law of Excluded Middle — Worked Proof **Claim:** For standard fuzzy operators, $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^c \neq X$. **Proof by counterexample:** Let $\tilde{A} = \left\{\frac{0.4}{x_1}\right\}$. Compute complement: $$\tilde{A}^c = \left\{\frac{0.6}{x_1}\right\}$$ Compute standard union: $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^c}(x_1) = \max(0.4,\ 0.6) = 0.6$$ But for the universe $X$, the membership of $x_1$ in $X$ is $1$, not $0.6$. Therefore: $$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^c \neq X \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KEY TAKEAWAY FOR KTU ESSAYS** > This is the most profound difference between classical and fuzzy set theory. The "excluded middle" is the philosophical statement that an element is *either* in a set *or* its complement — never both, never neither. In fuzzy logic, an element with grade $0.4$ is partially in $\tilde{A}$ and partially in $\tilde{A}^c$ (grade $0.6$), violating the strict either/or dichotomy. --- ## 3.5 Distributivity Verification — Standard Fuzzy Operators **Claim:** $\tilde{A} \cap (\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C}) = (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}) \cup (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{C})$ **Proof:** Let $a, b, c \in [0,1]$. LHS: $$\mu_{\text{LHS}}(x) = \min(a,\ \max(b, c))$$ RHS: $$\mu_{\text{RHS}}(x) = \max(\min(a, b),\ \min(a, c))$$ We need to show $\min(a, \max(b, c)) = \max(\min(a, b), \min(a, c))$. **Case 1:** $a \ge b$ and $a \ge c$. Then $\max(b,c) \le a$, so LHS = $\max(b,c)$. RHS = $\max(b, c)$. ✅ **Case 2:** $a \le b$ and $a \le c$. Then LHS = $\min(a, \max(b,c)) = a$ (since $\max(b,c) \ge a$). RHS = $\max(a, a) = a$. ✅ **Case 3:** $b \le a \le c$. Then $\max(b,c) = c$, LHS = $\min(a, c) = a$. RHS = $\max(b, a) = a$. ✅ (Other cases symmetric.) Therefore distributivity **holds** for standard fuzzy operations. $\blacksquare$ # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Fuzzy Set Operations Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["FUZZY SET
OPERATIONS"] nodeB["PRIMITIVE
OPERATIONS"] nodeC["DERIVED
OPERATIONS"] nodeD["Intersection
AND operation"] nodeE["Union
OR operation"] nodeF["Complement
NOT operation"] nodeG["T-Norm Family"] nodeH["T-Conorm Family"] nodeI["Negator Family"] nodeJ["Standard Min"] nodeK["Algebraic Product"] nodeL["Bounded Product"] nodeM["Drastic Product"] nodeN["Standard Max"] nodeO["Algebraic Sum"] nodeP["Bounded Sum"] nodeQ["Drastic Sum"] nodeR["Standard Negator
1 minus a"] nodeS["Sugeno Negator"] nodeT["Yager Negator"] nodeU["Difference
A minus B"] nodeV["XOR Symmetric Diff"] nodeW["Cartesian Product"] nodeX["Power Set
Composition"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeJ nodeG --> nodeK nodeG --> nodeL nodeG --> nodeM nodeH --> nodeN nodeH --> nodeO nodeH --> nodeP nodeH --> nodeQ nodeI --> nodeR nodeI --> nodeS nodeI --> nodeT nodeC --> nodeU nodeC --> nodeV nodeC --> nodeW nodeC --> nodeX style nodeA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeB fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeC fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeD fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeE fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeF fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Element-wise Operation Flow ```mermaid graph LR nodeIn1["Membership Value a
from set A"] nodeIn2["Membership Value b
from set B"] nodeOp["OPERATOR STAR
Norm Family"] nodeOut["Resulting
Membership"] nodeCon["Constraint
Result in 0 to 1"] nodeIn1 --> nodeOp nodeIn2 --> nodeOp nodeOp --> nodeCon nodeCon --> nodeOut style nodeOp fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeOut fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — T-Norm Ordering (KTU Standard Result) ```mermaid graph LR nodeD["Drastic T-Norm
Smallest"] nodeB["Bounded Product
Lukasiewicz"] nodeA["Algebraic Product"] nodeM["Standard Min
Largest"] nodeEq["Relationship: a AND b"] nodeD --> nodeEq nodeB --> nodeEq nodeA --> nodeEq nodeM --> nodeEq style nodeD fill:#f4b084,stroke:#000 style nodeM fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Property Verification Matrix ```mermaid graph TD nodeP["PROPERTIES OF
FUZZY OPERATIONS"] nodeP1["Commutativity
Holds for all"] nodeP2["Associativity
Holds for all"] nodeP3["Distributivity
Holds for standard"] nodeP4["De Morgans Laws
Holds for standard"] nodeP5["Idempotence
Holds for all"] nodeP6["Law of Excluded Middle
FAILS for fuzzy"] nodeP7["Law of Non-Contradiction
FAILS for fuzzy"] nodeP --> nodeP1 nodeP --> nodeP2 nodeP --> nodeP3 nodeP --> nodeP4 nodeP --> nodeP5 nodeP --> nodeP6 nodeP --> nodeP7 style nodeP6 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeP7 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeP1 fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeP2 fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeP3 fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeP4 fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeP5 fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fuzzy Decision Engine ```mermaid graph TD nodeStep1["INPUT
Crisp Sensor Data
Example: temp = 28 C"] nodeStep2["FUZZIFICATION
Map to Membership Grades
mu_cold, mu_warm, mu_hot"] nodeStep3["RULE BASE
IF warm THEN medium fan"] nodeStep4["INFERENCE ENGINE
Apply T-Norm / T-Conorm
Compute Rule Activation"] nodeStep5["AGGREGATION
Combine Rule Outputs
Using S-Norm Max"] nodeStep6["DEFUZZIFICATION
Convert Fuzzy Output
to Crisp Action
Example: fan speed 60 percent"] nodeStep7["ACTUATOR OUTPUT"] nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2 nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3 nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4 nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5 nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6 nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7 style nodeStep2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeStep4 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeStep5 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeStep6 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question] **Define fuzzy complement and state any two properties it must satisfy.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **fuzzy complement** of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$, denoted $\bar{A}$ or $\tilde{A}^c$, assigns to each element $x \in X$ the membership grade: $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \quad \text{(Standard Negator)}$$ **Two essential properties:** 1. **Boundary Conditions:** $\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(x) = 0$ when $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$, and $\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(x) = 1$ when $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0$. This ensures the empty set complements to the whole universe and vice versa. 2. **Involution (Double Complement Law):** $\overline{\overline{\tilde{A}}} = \tilde{A}$, i.e., $1 - (1 - a) = a$ for all $a \in [0,1]$. > **[Mark Allocation: 1 for definition + 1 for each property = 3 marks]** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question] **List any three fuzzy intersection (T-norm) operators and write their formulas.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Given two membership values $a, b \in [0,1]$: 1. **Standard Intersection (Minimum):** $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(a, b)$ 2. **Algebraic Product:** $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = a \cdot b$ 3. **Bounded Product (Łukasiewicz):** $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(0, a + b - 1)$ > **[Mark Allocation: 1 mark per operator with formula = 3 marks]** --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]** **(a)** *Define fuzzy set. Given two fuzzy sets* $$\tilde{A} = \left\{\frac{0.3}{x_1},\ \frac{0.8}{x_2},\ \frac{0.5}{x_3}\right\}, \quad \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.7}{x_1},\ \frac{0.2}{x_2},\ \frac{0.9}{x_3}\right\}$$ *compute $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ and $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ using (i) standard (min/max) operators and (ii) algebraic operators.* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** *State and verify De Morgan's Laws for fuzzy sets using the result obtained in part (a). Also discuss whether the Law of Excluded Middle holds in fuzzy set theory with a suitable counter-example.* **(7 Marks)** --- #### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A ### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution [7 Marks] **Given:** $$\tilde{A} = \left\{\frac{0.3}{x_1},\ \frac{0.8}{x_2},\ \frac{0.5}{x_3}\right\}, \quad \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.7}{x_1},\ \frac{0.2}{x_2},\ \frac{0.9}{x_3}\right\}$$ **Membership value pairs $(a, b)$ for each element:** | Element | $a = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | $b = \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$ | |---|---|---| | $x_1$ | 0.3 | 0.7 | | $x_2$ | 0.8 | 0.2 | | $x_3$ | 0.5 | 0.9 | #### (i) Standard Operators (Min / Max) **Union** $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(a, b)$: $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_1) = \max(0.3,\ 0.7) = 0.7$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_2) = \max(0.8,\ 0.2) = 0.8$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_3) = \max(0.5,\ 0.9) = 0.9$$ $$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.7}{x_1},\ \frac{0.8}{x_2},\ \frac{0.9}{x_3}\right\}$$ **Intersection** $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(a, b)$: $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_1) = \min(0.3,\ 0.7) = 0.3$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_2) = \min(0.8,\ 0.2) = 0.2$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_3) = \min(0.5,\ 0.9) = 0.5$$ $$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.3}{x_1},\ \frac{0.2}{x_2},\ \frac{0.5}{x_3}\right\}$$ #### (ii) Algebraic Operators **Union** $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = a + b - a \cdot b$: $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_1) = 0.3 + 0.7 - (0.3)(0.7) = 1.0 - 0.21 = 0.79$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_2) = 0.8 + 0.2 - (0.8)(0.2) = 1.0 - 0.16 = 0.84$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_3) = 0.5 + 0.9 - (0.5)(0.9) = 1.4 - 0.45 = 0.95$$ $$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} \ (\text{algebraic}) = \left\{\frac{0.79}{x_1},\ \frac{0.84}{x_2},\ \frac{0.95}{x_3}\right\}$$ **Intersection** $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = a \cdot b$: $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_1) = (0.3)(0.7) = 0.21$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_2) = (0.8)(0.2) = 0.16$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_3) = (0.5)(0.9) = 0.45$$ $$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} \ (\text{algebraic}) = \left\{\frac{0.21}{x_1},\ \frac{0.16}{x_2},\ \frac{0.45}{x_3}\right\}$$ > **[Marks: 1 definition + 1 for (i) standard operations × 2 = 2 + 1 for algebraic sum + 1 for algebraic product + 1 for final results = 7 marks]** --- ### Part (b) — De Morgan's Laws Verification [7 Marks] **Statement of De Morgan's Laws (KTU Expected Form):** $$\overline{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}} = \tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c \quad \text{and} \quad \overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \tilde{A}^c \cap \tilde{B}^c$$ **Verification using standard intersection from Part (a):** Step 1: Compute complements: $$\tilde{A}^c = \left\{\frac{0.7}{x_1},\ \frac{0.2}{x_2},\ \frac{0.5}{x_3}\right\}$$ $$\tilde{B}^c = \left\{\frac{0.3}{x_1},\ \frac{0.8}{x_2},\ \frac{0.1}{x_3}\right\}$$ Step 2: Compute LHS — complement of intersection: $$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.3}{x_1},\ \frac{0.2}{x_2},\ \frac{0.5}{x_3}\right\}$$ $$\overline{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}} = \left\{\frac{0.7}{x_1},\ \frac{0.8}{x_2},\ \frac{0.5}{x_3}\right\}$$ Step 3: Compute RHS — union of complements: $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c}(x_1) = \max(0.7,\ 0.3) = 0.7$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c}(x_2) = \max(0.2,\ 0.8) = 0.8$$ $$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c}(x_3) = \max(0.5,\ 0.1) = 0.5$$ $$\tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c = \left\{\frac{0.7}{x_1},\ \frac{0.8}{x_2},\ \frac{0.5}{x_3}\right\}$$ **Since LHS = RHS, De Morgan's First Law is verified.** ✅ Similar verification can be performed for the second law. **Failure of Law of Excluded Middle:** Consider $\tilde{A} = \left\{\frac{0.4}{x_1}\right\}$. Then $\tilde{A}^c = \left\{\frac{0.6}{x_1}\right\}$. $$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^c = \left\{\frac{\max(0.4,\ 0.6)}{x_1}\right\} = \left\{\frac{0.6}{x_1}\right\}$$ But for the universal set $X$, $\mu_X(x_1) = 1$. Since $0.6 \neq 1$, the Law of Excluded Middle **fails** for fuzzy sets. **Reason:** Fuzzy logic allows *graded* membership, so an element can simultaneously partially belong to both $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{A}^c$, violating the classical either/or dichotomy. > **[Marks: 2 for statement + 3 for verification + 2 for discussion of excluded middle = 7 marks]** --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]** **(a)** *Define a T-norm and T-conorm. Explain with formulas the four standard T-norms (intersection operators): minimum, algebraic product, bounded product, and drastic product. State which T-norm gives the largest and which gives the smallest result for any $a, b \in (0, 1)$.* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** *Given the fuzzy sets* $\tilde{A} = \left\{\frac{0.4}{x_1},\ \frac{0.9}{x_2},\ \frac{0.1}{x_3},\ \frac{0.6}{x_4}\right\}$ *and* $\tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.7}{x_1},\ \frac{0.3}{x_2},\ \frac{0.8}{x_3},\ \frac{0.2}{x_4}\right\}$, *compute:* *(i) Bounded sum $\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}$,* *(ii) Bounded difference,* *(iii) Algebraic product.* *Also state two properties that hold for fuzzy set operations but fail for classical Boolean algebra.* **(7 Marks)** --- #### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B ### Part (a) — T-Norm and T-Conorm Definitions [7 Marks] **T-Norm Definition:** A **T-norm** $\top : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ is a binary operation satisfying: - **Boundary:** $\top(a, 1) = a$ and $\top(a, 0) = 0$ - **Commutativity:** $\top(a, b) = \top(b, a)$ - **Monotonicity:** $a \le c$ and $b \le d \Rightarrow \top(a, b) \le \top(c, d)$ - **Associativity:** $\top(a, \top(b, c)) = \top(\top(a, b), c)$ **T-Conorm Definition:** A **T-conorm** $\bot : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ is a binary operation satisfying: - **Boundary:** $\bot(a, 0) = a$ and $\bot(a, 1) = 1$ - Commutativity, Monotonicity, Associativity as above. **The Four Standard T-Norms** (for $a, b \in [0,1]$): | T-Norm | Formula | |---|---| | **Drastic Product** (smallest) | $T_D(a,b) = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } b = 1 \\ b & \text{if } a = 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | | **Bounded Product** (Łukasiewicz) | $T_B(a,b) = \max(0,\ a + b - 1)$ | | **Algebraic Product** | $T_A(a,b) = a \cdot b$ | | **Minimum** (largest) | $T_M(a,b) = \min(a,\ b)$ | **Ordering:** For $a, b \in (0, 1)$ (non-trivial case): $$T_D(a,b) \le T_B(a,b) \le T_A(a,b) \le T_M(a,b)$$ The **Minimum (standard) intersection gives the largest value**, while the **Drastic Product gives the smallest value**. > **[Marks: 1.5 for T-norm + 1.5 for T-conorm + 3 for four formulas + 1 for ordering = 7 marks]** --- ### Part (b) — Operations on Fuzzy Sets [7 Marks] **Given:** $$\tilde{A} = \left\{\frac{0.4}{x_1},\ \frac{0.9}{x_2},\ \frac{0.1}{x_3},\ \frac{0.6}{x_4}\right\}, \quad \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.7}{x_1},\ \frac{0.3}{x_2},\ \frac{0.8}{x_3},\ \frac{0.2}{x_4}\right\}$$ #### (i) Bounded Sum $\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}$ — Formula: $\mu(x) = \min(1, a + b)$ | Element | $a$ | $b$ | $a+b$ | $\min(1, a+b)$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | $x_1$ | 0.4 | 0.7 | 1.1 | **1.0** | | $x_2$ | 0.9 | 0.3 | 1.2 | **1.0** | | $x_3$ | 0.1 | 0.8 | 0.9 | **0.9** | | $x_4$ | 0.6 | 0.2 | 0.8 | **0.8** | $$\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{1.0}{x_1},\ \frac{1.0}{x_2},\ \frac{0.9}{x_3},\ \frac{0.8}{x_4}\right\}$$ #### (ii) Bounded Difference $\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B}$ — Formula: $\mu(x) = \max(0, a - b)$ | Element | $a$ | $b$ | $a-b$ | $\max(0, a-b)$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | $x_1$ | 0.4 | 0.7 | -0.3 | **0.0** | | $x_2$ | 0.9 | 0.3 | 0.6 | **0.6** | | $x_3$ | 0.1 | 0.8 | -0.7 | **0.0** | | $x_4$ | 0.6 | 0.2 | 0.4 | **0.4** | $$\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.0}{x_1},\ \frac{0.6}{x_2},\ \frac{0.0}{x_3},\ \frac{0.4}{x_4}\right\}$$ #### (iii) Algebraic Product $\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}$ — Formula: $\mu(x) = a \cdot b$ | Element | $a$ | $b$ | $a \cdot b$ | |---|---|---|---| | $x_1$ | 0.4 | 0.7 | **0.28** | | $x_2$ | 0.9 | 0.3 | **0.27** | | $x_3$ | 0.1 | 0.8 | **0.08** | | $x_4$ | 0.6 | 0.2 | **0.12** | $$\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B} = \left\{\frac{0.28}{x_1},\ \frac{0.27}{x_2},\ \frac{0.08}{x_3},\ \frac{0.12}{x_4}\right\}$$ #### Properties That Hold for Fuzzy but **Fail** for Classical Boolean Algebra: 1. **Partial Membership:** A fuzzy element can have a membership grade strictly between $0$ and $1$ (e.g., $0.4$), whereas classical sets force $0$ or $1$. 2. **De Morgan's Law generalised:** While De Morgan's Laws hold in both algebras, in fuzzy logic the standard (min/max) operators maintain them — but in Boolean algebra, *only* the classical crisp versions are valid. Fuzzy generalisation allows continuous operations. 3. **Failure of Excluded Middle:** In Boolean, $A \cup A^c = U$ always. In fuzzy (standard), $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^c \neq X$, as demonstrated earlier. 4. **Failure of Non-Contradiction:** In Boolean, $A \cap A^c = \emptyset$. In fuzzy (standard), $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{A}^c \neq \emptyset$ for partial memberships. > **[Marks: 1.5 each for (i), (ii), (iii) = 4.5 + 2.5 for properties = 7 marks]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS** > > 1. **Confusing algebraic product and bounded product** — they are NOT the same. Algebraic is $a \cdot b$; Bounded is $\max(0, a+b-1)$. **Cost: up to 4 marks lost.** > 2. **Forgetting the casewise definition of drastic operators** — examiners expect the conditional (if-then-else) form, not just a single formula. Writing only "$= 0$" without specifying the boundary conditions costs full marks. > 3. **Stating that the Law of Excluded Middle holds in fuzzy logic** — this is a **conceptual error**. Always state explicitly that it **fails** for standard operators. > 4. **Miscalculating bounded sum when $a + b > 1$** — students forget to apply the $\min(1, \cdot)$ cap and write values like $1.2$ as the membership. Membership MUST lie in $[0, 1]$. > 5. **Forgetting to define both T-norm AND T-conorm** in part (a) of long-answer questions — KTU strictly requires both definitions for full marks. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — READ THIS BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL** - ✅ A **fuzzy set** is defined by a membership function $\mu : X \to [0, 1]$. - ✅ The **three primitive fuzzy operations** are: **Complement (NOT)**, **Intersection (AND)**, **Union (OR)**. - ✅ The **standard (canonical)** fuzzy operators are: $\mu^c = 1 - a$, $\mu_{\cap} = \min(a,b)$, $\mu_{\cup} = \max(a,b)$. - ✅ **Algebraic intersection** = $a \cdot b$; **Algebraic union** = $a + b - ab$. - ✅ **Bounded intersection** (Łukasiewicz) = $\max(0, a+b-1)$; **Bounded union** = $\min(1, a+b)$. - ✅ **Drastic intersection** = $a$ if $b=1$, $b$ if $a=1$, else $0$. - ✅ **Drastic union** = $a$ if $b=0$, $b$ if $a=0$, else $1$. - ✅ **T-norm ordering:** Drastic $\le$ Bounded $\le$ Algebraic $\le$ Min. - ✅ **T-conorm ordering:** Max $\le$ Algebraic Sum $\le$ Bounded Sum $\le$ Drastic Sum. - ✅ **Properties that HOLD for standard fuzzy ops:** Commutativity, Associativity, Distributivity, Idempotence, De Morgan's Laws, Involution. - ✅ **Properties that FAIL:** Law of Excluded Middle, Law of Non-Contradiction. - ✅ **De Morgan's Laws:** $\overline{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}} = \tilde{A}^c \cup \tilde{B}^c$ and $\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \tilde{A}^c \cap \tilde{B}^c$. - ✅ **Membership values MUST always lie in $[0, 1]$** — always apply the $\min(1, \cdot)$ / $\max(0, \cdot)$ clamps when using bounded operators. - ✅ The failure of Excluded Middle is the **defining philosophical distinction** between fuzzy and classical set theory — **mention this in every essay-type answer for bonus marks**. - ✅ **Sugeno and Yager complements** are parametrised families useful when standard $1-a$ does not capture the desired negation semantics. - ✅ For KTU 14-mark problems, always: (1) show element-wise calculations, (2) tabulate intermediate values, (3) state the operator formula before applying, (4) verify De Morgan's if the question allows.

topics.title: Optimization of Fuzzy Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET755 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization of Fuzzy Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fuzzy Optimization** is a class of mathematical programming techniques that explicitly incorporates **imprecision, vagueness, and linguistic uncertainty** into the formulation of decision-making problems. Unlike classical optimization where parameters, constraints, and objectives are assumed to be precisely known real numbers, fuzzy optimization allows these elements to be represented as **fuzzy sets** characterized by **membership functions** $\mu(x) \in [0, 1]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Fuzzy Optimization refers to the process of finding optimal decisions in problems where the objective function, constraints, or parameters are described by fuzzy sets rather than crisp numerical values. The goal is to maximize *overall satisfaction* rather than a single scalar objective.

The three principal formulations studied in **PEMET755 Module 4** are:

1. **Fuzzy Linear Programming (FLP)** — Extension of LP with fuzzy coefficients/constraints.
2. **Fuzzy Multi-Objective Linear Programming (FMOLP)** — Multiple fuzzy objectives.
3. **Fuzzy Dynamic Programming (FDP)** — Sequential decision-making under fuzziness.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Lunch Budget Problem"**
> Imagine you tell your friend: *"I want a meal that is **fairly cheap**, **reasonably tasty**, and **somewhat healthy**."* A classical optimization model cannot process these vague linguistic terms. A fuzzy system interprets each term as a **membership function**: a cheap meal might have $\mu = 1$ below ₹100 and gradually drop to $\mu = 0$ above ₹200. Optimization then finds the dish that **simultaneously maximizes the minimum satisfaction** across all fuzzy goals.

This is the **Bellman–Zadeh principle of optimality in fuzzy environments** — the optimal decision is the one that *best balances all fuzzy goals and constraints together*.

---

### 1.3 Foundational Vocabulary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Fuzzy Set** $\tilde{A}$ | A set whose elements have graded membership in $[0,1]$ |
| **Membership Function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | Degree to which $x$ belongs to $\tilde{A}$ |
| **Support** | Set where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0$ |
| **Core** | Set where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$ |
| **α-cut** | $A_{\alpha} = \{x : \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$ |
| **Crisp (Classical) Set** | Membership only $0$ or $1$ |
| **Fuzzy Number** | Convex, normalized fuzzy set on $\mathbb{R}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Fuzzy optimization is widely applied in **supply chain logistics, power dispatch, portfolio selection, traffic control, inventory management, and production planning**, where real data is noisy or expert-driven.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bellman–Zadeh Principle (The "Why" of Fuzzy Optimization)

In a fuzzy environment, the **decision** $\tilde{D}$, the **goal** $\tilde{G}$, and the **constraints** $\tilde{C}$ are all fuzzy sets. The fuzzy decision is their **intersection**:

$$
\tilde{D} = \tilde{G} \cap \tilde{C}
$$

with membership function:

$$
\mu_{\tilde{D}}(x) = \min\{\mu_{\tilde{G}}(x),\; \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x)\}
$$

The **optimal decision** $x^{*}$ is the one that **maximizes this membership**:

$$
\mu_{\tilde{D}}(x^{*}) = \max_{x \in X} \min\{\mu_{\tilde{G}}(x),\; \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x)\}
$$

> This is the **Max–Min** formulation — the heart of fuzzy optimization.

---

### 2.2 Types of Fuzzy Linear Programming (FLP)

| Type | Fuzzy Element | Conversion Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1 — Symmetric FLP** | Both $\tilde{b}$ and objective fuzzy | Verdegay (1982) |
| **Type 2 — Asymmetric FLP** | Only RHS $\tilde{b}$ fuzzy | Zimmermann (1976) |
| **Type 3 — Fuzzy Coefficients** | $\tilde{c}, \tilde{A}, \tilde{b}$ all fuzzy | Multi-parametric |

---

### 2.3 Zimmermann's Fuzzy LP Method (Most Exam-Relevant)

**Crisp LP:**
$$
\max Z = c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax \leq b,\; x \geq 0
$$

**Fuzzy LP (Asymmetric):**
$$
\max Z \gtrsim c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax \lesssim \tilde{b},\; x \geq 0
$$

Here $\gtrsim$ and $\lesssim$ denote *fuzzy inequalities* — the decision-maker is willing to tolerate slight violations.

Membership functions (linear, decreasing):

$$
\mu_0(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & c^T x \geq Z^* \\ \dfrac{c^T x - Z^-}{Z^* - Z^-}, & Z^- \leq c^T x \leq Z^* \\ 0, & c^T x \leq Z^- \end{cases}
$$

$$
\mu_i(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & (Ax)_i \leq b_i \\ 1 - \dfrac{(Ax)_i - b_i}{p_i}, & b_i \leq (Ax)_i \leq b_i + p_i \\ 0, & (Ax)_i \geq b_i + p_i \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $Z^*$ = optimal value of crisp LP
- $Z^-$ = aspiration level (worst acceptable objective)
- $p_i$ = maximum tolerable violation in $i$-th constraint

---

### 2.4 Equivalent Crisp LP (Zimmermann's Transformation)

Introducing auxiliary variable $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$
\max \lambda
$$

subject to:

$$
\lambda (Z^* - Z^-) \leq c^T x - Z^-
$$

$$
\lambda \, p_i \leq b_i + p_i - (Ax)_i \quad \forall i
$$

$$
x \geq 0,\quad 0 \leq \lambda \leq 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Solving this crisp equivalent gives the optimal **compromise solution** with maximum satisfaction level $\lambda^{*}$.

---

### 2.5 Verdegay's Approach (Symmetric FLP)

For symmetric FLP with $\tilde{b} = (b, \bar{b})$ where $\bar{b} > b$:

For each $\alpha \in [0,1]$, solve crisp LP:

$$
\max Z = c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax \leq b + \alpha(\bar{b} - b),\; x \geq 0
$$

The fuzzy optimal solution is the **union of all $\alpha$-level solutions**.

---

### 2.6 Fuzzy Dynamic Programming (FDP)

**Bellman's Functional Equation in Fuzzy Environment:**

$$
\mu^*_{\tilde{D}}(s) = \max_{x_s} \min \Big\{ \mu_{\tilde{G}_k}(s, x_s),\; \mu^*_{\tilde{D}}(s+1) \Big\}
$$

Where $s$ is the state and $x_s$ is the decision at stage $s$.

> The recursion proceeds **backwards** (as in classical DP) but uses **min–max** operators over fuzzy membership values instead of additive cost values.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\tilde{A}$ | Fuzzy set |
| $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | Membership function, range $\vert 0, 1 \vert$ |
| $A_{\alpha}$ | α-cut of $\tilde{A}$ |
| $\lambda^{*}$ | Optimal satisfaction degree (Zimmermann) |
| $Z^*$ | Crisp LP optimum |
| $Z^-$ | Decision-maker's aspiration level |
| $p_i$ | Tolerance for $i$-th constraint |
| $\tilde{D} = \tilde{G} \cap \tilde{C}$ | Bellman–Zadeh decision |
| $\mu_{\tilde{D}} = \min\{\mu_{\tilde{G}}, \mu_{\tilde{C}}\}$ | Combined membership |
| $\max \min\{\cdot\}$ | Max–Min aggregation operator |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "MGM":** **M**embership, **G**oal, **M**ax-Min. The fuzzy optimum is the **Max** of the **Min** of memberships across goals and constraints.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Crisp LP to Zimmermann's Fuzzy LP

**Step 1 — Start with Crisp LP:**

$$
\max Z = c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax \leq b,\; x \geq 0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the crisp optimum** $Z^* = \max c^T x$ subject to $Ax \leq b$.

**Step 3 — Determine aspiration level $Z^-$:**
The decision-maker states a target value below which the solution is unacceptable. Typical choice:
$$
Z^- = c^T x \quad \text{at the worst vertex feasible under} \quad Ax \leq b + p
$$

**Step 4 — Define membership functions (linear, decreasing):**

$$
\mu_0(x) = \frac{c^T x - Z^-}{Z^* - Z^-}
$$

$$
\mu_i(x) = 1 - \frac{(Ax)_i - b_i}{p_i}, \quad b_i \leq (Ax)_i \leq b_i + p_i
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bellman–Zadeh Aggregation:**

$$
\mu_{\tilde{D}}(x) = \min\{\mu_0(x), \mu_1(x), \mu_2(x), \ldots, \mu_m(x)\}
$$

**Step 6 — Introduce auxiliary variable $\lambda$ such that:**

$$
\lambda \leq \mu_0(x) \quad \text{and} \quad \lambda \leq \mu_i(x)\;\; \forall i
$$

This yields the equivalent crisp LP:

$$
\max \lambda
$$

subject to:

$$
\lambda (Z^* - Z^-) \leq c^T x - Z^-
$$

$$
\lambda \, p_i + (Ax)_i \leq b_i + p_i \quad \forall i
$$

$$
x \geq 0,\quad \lambda \geq 0
$$

**Step 7 — Solve the crisp LP using Simplex.** The optimal $\lambda^*$ represents the **maximum achievable satisfaction**. The corresponding $x^*$ is the **fuzzy optimal solution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Derivation.** Every step shown explicitly; no algebraic transition skipped.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Crisp LP:**
$$
\max Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2
$$
$$
2x_1 + x_2 \leq 40
$$
$$
x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 30
$$
$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

**Step 1 — Solve Crisp LP (Simplex):**

Vertices: $(0,0), (20,0), (0,10), (15, 5)$.
- $Z(20,0) = 100$
- $Z(0,10) = 40$
- $Z(15,5) = 5(15)+4(5) = 95$

$$
\boxed{Z^* = 100 \text{ at } x^* = (20,0)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define tolerances:**
Aspiration level $Z^- = 50$. Tolerances $p_1 = 10$, $p_2 = 6$.

**Step 3 — Write Zimmermann's Crisp Equivalent:**

$$
\max \lambda
$$

$$
50\lambda \leq 5x_1 + 4x_2 - 50
$$

$$
10\lambda + 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 50
$$

$$
6\lambda + x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 36
$$

$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0,\; \lambda \geq 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solution:** Solving via Simplex gives $\lambda^* = 0.875$ with $x_1 = 18.75$, $x_2 = 3.125$.

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- $87.5\%$ of the maximum achievable satisfaction is reached.
- The solution is a **compromise** between objective aspiration and constraint tolerances.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Fuzzy LP Approaches

| Approach | Fuzzy Element | Result Type | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bellman–Zadeh (1970) | Goals + Constraints | Max-Min solution | Low |
| Zimmermann (1976) | Asymmetric RHS | Crisp LP equivalent | Medium |
| Verdegay (1982) | Symmetric RHS | Parametric LP family | Medium |
| Werners (1987) | Interactive | Iterative | High |
| Slowinski (1990) | Fuzzy coefficients | Multi-parametric | High |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Bellman–Zadeh principle of optimality in a fuzzy environment.*

**Model Answer:**
In a fuzzy environment, the fuzzy decision $\tilde{D}$ is the intersection of the fuzzy goal $\tilde{G}$ and fuzzy constraints $\tilde{C}$, i.e.,
$$
\tilde{D} = \tilde{G} \cap \tilde{C}
$$
with membership function $\mu_{\tilde{D}}(x) = \min\{\mu_{\tilde{G}}(x),\; \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x)\}$. The optimal decision $x^{*}$ maximizes this combined membership:

$$
x^{*} = \arg\max_{x} \min\{\mu_{\tilde{G}}(x),\; \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x)\}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is an $\alpha$-cut of a fuzzy set? Give its significance in fuzzy optimization.*

**Model Answer:**
The $\alpha$-cut of fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is:
$$
A_{\alpha} = \{x : \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\},\quad \alpha \in [0,1]
$$
**Significance:** It converts a fuzzy set into a family of crisp sets, allowing classical optimization tools (Simplex, etc.) to be applied at each $\alpha$-level. This is the basis of **Verdegay's parametric approach** to fuzzy LP.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Zimmermann's Fuzzy LP Method (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set A]**

A company manufactures two products $P_1$ and $P_2$. Profits per unit are **₹5** and **₹4** respectively. Resources needed are:

| Resource | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | Available |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material A | 2 | 1 | **40 (fuzzy, tolerance 5)** |
| Labour | 1 | 3 | **30 (fuzzy, tolerance 4)** |

The manager aspires to a profit of **₹70** but will accept **₹50**. Formulate and solve the fuzzy LP using Zimmermann's method.

**(a) Crisp LP Solution and Membership Function Construction (7 marks)**
**(b) Conversion to Equivalent Crisp LP and Final Answer (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Crisp LP:

$$
\max Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2
$$
$$
2x_1 + x_2 \leq 40
$$
$$
x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 30
$$
$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

Vertices: $(0,0), (20,0), (0,10), (15,5)$.
- $Z(20,0) = 100$
- $Z(0,10) = 40$
- $Z(15,5) = 95$

Crisp optimum: $Z^* = 100$ at $(20,0)$.

Membership functions:

$$
\mu_0(x) = \frac{5x_1 + 4x_2 - 50}{100 - 50} = \frac{5x_1 + 4x_2 - 50}{50}
$$

$$
\mu_1(x) = 1 - \frac{2x_1 + x_2 - 40}{5} = \frac{45 - 2x_1 - x_2}{5}
$$

$$
\mu_2(x) = 1 - \frac{x_1 + 3x_2 - 30}{4} = \frac{34 - x_1 - 3x_2}{4}
$$

**(b)** Equivalent Crisp LP with auxiliary $\lambda$:

$$
\max \lambda
$$

$$
50\lambda \leq 5x_1 + 4x_2 - 50
$$

$$
5\lambda \leq 45 - 2x_1 - x_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 5\lambda + 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 45
$$

$$
4\lambda \leq 34 - x_1 - 3x_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 4\lambda + x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 34
$$

$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0,\quad \lambda \geq 0
$$

Solving via Simplex (graphical/numerical): at intersection of active constraints,

$$
5\lambda + 2x_1 + x_2 = 45
$$
$$
4\lambda + x_1 + 3x_2 = 34
$$
$$
50\lambda = 5x_1 + 4x_2 - 50
$$

Solving yields $\lambda^* \approx 0.78$, $x_1 \approx 17.1$, $x_2 \approx 3.4$.

**Conclusion:** The company achieves **78%** satisfaction with compromise production plan $x_1 \approx 17$, $x_2 \approx 3$.

---

#### **Question B — Fuzzy Dynamic Programming (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set B — Internal Choice]**

A two-stage investment problem has three investment options per stage. The fuzzy returns are given by fuzzy sets $\tilde{R}_1 = \{(S1, 0.9), (S2, 0.6), (S3, 0.3)\}$ and $\tilde{R}_2 = \{(S1, 0.8), (S2, 0.5), (S3, 0.7)\}$. Apply the **fuzzy Bellman equation** to determine the optimal investment policy.

**(a) Define the Fuzzy Bellman Equation and state its functional form (7 marks)**
**(b) Solve backwards from Stage 2 to Stage 1 using Min–Max aggregation (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Fuzzy Bellman functional equation:

$$
\mu^*_{\tilde{D}}(s) = \max_{x_s \in X_s} \min \Big\{\mu_{\tilde{R}_k}(s, x_s),\; \mu^*_{\tilde{D}}(s+1)\Big\}
$$

The recursion proceeds backwards; the optimal value at the final stage is the membership of the chosen decision.

**(b)** **Stage 2 (Final):**

$$
\mu^*_{\tilde{D}}(s, x_2) = \mu_{\tilde{R}_2}(x_2)
$$

| $x_2$ | $\mu_{\tilde{R}_2}$ |
|---|---|
| S1 | 0.8 |
| S2 | 0.5 |
| S3 | 0.7 |

**Stage 1 (Backward Recursion):**

For each $x_1 \in \{S1, S2, S3\}$ and each possible $x_2$:

$$
\mu_{\tilde{D}}(x_1, x_2) = \min\{\mu_{\tilde{R}_1}(x_1),\; \mu_{\tilde{R}_2}(x_2)\}
$$

| $x_1 \backslash x_2$ | S1 (0.8) | S2 (0.5) | S3 (0.7) |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 (0.9) | **0.8** | 0.5 | 0.7 |
| S2 (0.6) | 0.6 | **0.5** | 0.6 |
| S3 (0.3) | 0.3 | 0.3 | **0.3** |

For each $x_1$, take $\max_{x_2}$:

- $x_1 = S1$: $\max = 0.8$ at $x_2 = S1$
- $x_1 = S2$: $\max = 0.6$ at $x_2 = S1$ or $S3$
- $x_1 = S3$: $\max = 0.3$ at any $x_2$

Overall optimum: $x_1^* = S1,\; x_2^* = S1$ with satisfaction $\lambda^* = 0.8$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing $Z^*$ and $Z^-$**: $Z^*$ is the *crisp LP optimum*, $Z^-$ is the *decision-maker's aspiration*. Many students swap them, causing the entire membership function to invert.
> 2. **Forgetting the auxiliary variable $\lambda$**: The equivalent crisp LP must include $\lambda$ explicitly with upper bound 1. Marks deducted if $\lambda$ is omitted.
> 3. **Incorrect aggregation**: Always use **Min** for combining memberships (intersection), then **Max** for selection (optimization). Mixing these is a 4-mark penalty.
> 4. **α-cut omission in Verdegay's method**: Students forget that the union over $\alpha \in [0,1]$ produces the fuzzy solution; a single α-level does not suffice.
> 5. **Sign errors in membership function construction**: Linear decreasing functions for constraints require careful $1 - \frac{\text{violation}}{\text{tolerance}}$ form.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Bellman–Zadeh principle**: $\tilde{D} = \tilde{G} \cap \tilde{C}$; optimal $x$ maximizes $\min\{\mu_G, \mu_C\}$.
- ✅ **Three pillars of fuzzy optimization**: Fuzzy goal, Fuzzy constraints, Fuzzy parameters.
- ✅ **Zimmermann's method** = Asymmetric FLP converted to crisp LP via auxiliary variable $\lambda$.
- ✅ **Verdegay's method** = Parametric LP solved at each $\alpha$-level; fuzzy solution is union.
- ✅ **$\alpha$-cut** $A_{\alpha} = \{x : \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$ — bridges fuzzy and crisp worlds.
- ✅ **Membership function for objective** (Zimmermann): $\mu_0(x) = \frac{c^T x - Z^-}{Z^* - Z^-}$.
- ✅ **Membership function for constraint $i$**: $\mu_i(x) = 1 - \frac{(Ax)_i - b_i}{p_i}$.
- ✅ **Fuzzy DP uses Min–Max recursion**, not additive cost minimization.
- ✅ **λ\*** represents **maximum achievable satisfaction** degree (always in $[0,1]$).
- ✅ **Engineering Applications**: Power dispatch, supply chain, portfolio selection, inventory, production planning.
- ✅ **Exam-relevant**: Zimmermann's crisp equivalent formulation (highest frequency in KTU papers).
- ✅ **Common trap**: Forgetting $\lambda$ upper bound = 1.
- ✅ **Mnemonic**: **"MGM-Max-Min"** → Membership, Goal, Max-Min aggregation.
